title: Short title, extent, commencement and application
text: (1) This Act may be called the Companies Act, 2013.

sub_structure.2: (2) It extends to the whole of India.
sub_structure.4_a: (4) The provisions of this Act shall apply to- (a) companies incorporated under this Act or under any previous company law;

plain_english_summary: Establishes the name of the Act, its application across India, and confirms it applies to all companies incorporated under it or previous company laws.

title: Definitions
text: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,-

sub_structure.20: (20) "company" means a company incorporated under this Act or under any previous company law;
sub_structure.62: (62) "One Person Company" means a company which has only one person as a member;
sub_structure.68: (68) "private company" means a company having a minimum paid-up share capital... and which by its articles,- (i) restricts the right to transfer its shares; (ii) except in case of One Person Company, limits the number of its members to two hundred... (iii) prohibits any invitation to the public to subscribe for any securities...
sub_structure.69: (69) "promoter" means a person- (a) who has been named as such in a prospectus... or (b) who has control over the affairs of the company...
sub_structure.71: (71) "public company" means a company which- (a) is not a private company;

plain_english_summary: Defines crucial corporate terms for founders. It establishes what legally constitutes a 'Private Company' (limited to 200 members, cannot sell shares to the public) versus a 'Public Company' or a 'One Person Company', and legally defines who is considered a 'Promoter'.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Formation of company
text: (1) A company may be formed for any lawful purpose by- (a) seven or more persons, where the company to be formed is to be a public company; (b) two or more persons, where the company to be formed is to be a private company; or (c) one person, where the company to be formed is to be One Person Company...

sub_structure.2: (2) A company formed under sub-section (1) may be either- (a) a company limited by shares; or (b) a company limited by guarantee; or (c) an unlimited company.

plain_english_summary: Outlines the minimum founder requirements to start a company: at least 2 people for a private startup, 7 for a public company, or 1 for a One Person Company. The company can be limited by shares, guarantee, or be unlimited.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Members severally liable in certain cases
text: If at any time the number of members of a company is reduced, in the case of a public company, below seven, in the case of a private company, below two, and the company carries on business for more than six months while the number of members is so reduced, every person who is a member of the company during the time that it so carries on business after those six months and is cognisant of the fact... shall be severally liable for the payment of the whole debts of the company contracted during that time...
plain_english_summary: If a private startup's co-founder leaves and the company operates with only 1 member for more than 6 months, that remaining member loses their 'limited liability' protection and becomes personally responsible for the company's debts.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Memorandum
text: (1) The memorandum of a company shall state- (a) the name of the company with the last word "Limited" in the case of a public limited company, or the last words "Private Limited" in the case of a private limited company... (b) the State in which the registered office... is to be situated; (c) the objects for which the company is proposed to be incorporated...

sub_structure.2: (2) The name stated in the memorandum shall not- (a) be identical with or resemble too nearly to the name of an existing company... or (b) be such that its use by the company- (i) will constitute an offence... or (ii) is undesirable in the opinion of the Central Government.
sub_structure.5_i: (5)(i) Upon receipt of an application... the Registrar may... reserve the name for a period of twenty days from the date of approval...

plain_english_summary: Dictates what must be included in the Memorandum of Association (MOA), including the startup's name, registered state, and its business objects. It also sets the rules for name reservations and prohibits copying existing company names.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Articles
text: (1) The articles of a company shall contain the regulations for management of the company.

sub_structure.3: (3) The articles may contain provisions for entrenchment to the effect that specified provisions of the articles may be altered only if conditions or procedures as that are more restrictive than those applicable in the case of a special resolution, are met or complied with.
sub_structure.4: (4) The provisions for entrenchment referred to in sub-section (3) shall only be made either on formation of a company, or by an amendment in the articles agreed to by all the members of the company in the case of a private company...

plain_english_summary: The Articles of Association (AOA) contain the internal rules for running the startup. Founders can include 'entrenchment' clauses, meaning certain critical rules can only be changed if 100% of the shareholders agree, protecting minority founders.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Act to override memorandum, articles, etc.
text: Save as otherwise expressly provided in this Act- (a) the provisions of this Act shall have effect notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in the memorandum or articles of a company, or in any agreement executed by it...

sub_structure.b: (b) any provision contained in the memorandum, articles, agreement or resolution shall, to the extent to which it is repugnant to the provisions of this Act, become or be void...

plain_english_summary: The Companies Act is the supreme law. If a startup drafts a Shareholders' Agreement, Founders' Agreement, or Articles of Association that conflict with this Act, those conflicting clauses are legally void and unenforceable.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Incorporation of company
text: (1) There shall be filed with the Registrar within whose jurisdiction the registered office of a company is proposed to be situated, the following documents and information for registration...

sub_structure.1_b: (b) a declaration in the prescribed form by an advocate, a chartered accountant... who is engaged in the formation of the company... that all the requirements of this Act... have been complied with;
sub_structure.5: (5) If any person furnishes any false or incorrect particulars of any information or suppresses any material information... in relation to the registration of a company, he shall be liable for action under section 447.
sub_structure.6: (6) Without prejudice to the provisions of sub-section (5) where, at any time after the incorporation of a company, it is proved that the company has been got incorporated by furnishing any false or incorrect information... the promoters, the persons named as the first directors... shall each be liable for action under section 447.

plain_english_summary: Lists the exact documents needed to register a startup. It carries severe warnings: if founders lie or submit fake documents during incorporation, they will be prosecuted for corporate fraud under Section 447, and the company can be shut down.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Formation of companies with charitable objects, etc.
text: (1) Where it is proved to the satisfaction of the Central Government that a person or an association of persons proposed to be registered under this Act as a limited company- (a) has in its objects the promotion of commerce, art, science, sports, education, research, social welfare, religion, charity, protection of environment or any such other object; (b) intends to apply its profits, if any, or other income in promoting its objects; and (c) intends to prohibit the payment of any dividend to its members... the Central Government may... allow that person... to be registered as a limited company... without the addition to its name of the word "Limited"...
plain_english_summary: Allows for the creation of 'Section 8' companies (non-profits). These companies must reinvest all profits into their charitable or research goals and are strictly banned from paying dividends to founders or shareholders.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Effect of registration
text: From the date of incorporation mentioned in the certificate of incorporation, such subscribers to the memorandum and all other persons, as may, from time to time, become members of the company, shall be a body corporate by the name contained in the memorandum, capable of exercising all the functions of an incorporated company under this Act and having perpetual succession with power to acquire, hold and dispose of property, both movable and immovable, tangible and intangible, to contract and to sue and be sued, by the said name.
plain_english_summary: The moment a startup gets its Certificate of Incorporation, it legally becomes an independent 'person' (a body corporate). It can own property, sign contracts, and sue or be sued in its own name, completely separate from its founders.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Effect of memorandum and articles
text: (1) Subject to the provisions of this Act, the memorandum and articles shall, when registered, bind the company and the members thereof to the same extent as if they respectively had been signed by the company and by each member, and contained covenants on its and his part to observe all the provisions of the memorandum and of the articles.

sub_structure.2: (2) All monies payable by any member to the company under the memorandum or articles shall be a debt due from him to the company.

plain_english_summary: Once registered, the MOA and AOA act as a legally binding contract between the company and its shareholders. Any money a founder or shareholder owes the company based on these documents is treated as a legal debt.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Commencement of business, etc.
text: (1) A company incorporated after the commencement of the Companies (Amendment) Act, 2019 and having a share capital shall not commence any business or exercise any borrowing powers unless- (a) a declaration is filed by a director within a period of one hundred and eighty days of the date of incorporation of the company... that every subscriber to the memorandum has paid the value of the shares agreed to be taken by him...

sub_structure.1_b: (b) the company has filed with the Registrar a verification of its registered office as provided in sub-section (2) of section 12.
sub_structure.2: (2) If any default is made in complying with the requirements of this section, the company shall be liable to a penalty of fifty thousand rupees and every officer who is in default shall be liable to a penalty of one thousand rupees for each day during which such default continues but not exceeding an amount of one lakh rupees.
sub_structure.3: (3) Where no declaration has been filed... within a period of one hundred and eighty days... the Registrar has reasonable cause to believe that the company is not carrying on any business or operations, he may... initiate action for the removal of the name of the company from the register of companies...

plain_english_summary: A startup cannot legally start operations or borrow money until the founders deposit their initial share capital into the company bank account and file a declaration within 180 days of incorporation. Failure to do this risks heavy fines and having the company struck off the register.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Registered office of company
text: (1) A company shall, within thirty days of its incorporation and at all times thereafter, have a registered office capable of receiving and acknowledging all communications and notices as may be addressed to it.

sub_structure.3_a: (a) paint or affix its name, and the address of its registered office... on the outside of every office or place in which its business is carried on, in a conspicuous position...
sub_structure.3_c: (c) get its name, address of its registered office and the Corporate Identity Number along with telephone number... e-mail and website addresses, if any, printed in all its business letters, billheads, letter papers and in all its notices and other official publications;
sub_structure.4: (4) Notice of every change of the situation of the registered office... shall be given to the Registrar within thirty days of the change, who shall record the same.
sub_structure.8: (8) If any default is made in complying with the requirements of this section, the company and every officer who is in default shall be liable to a penalty of one thousand rupees for every day during which the default continues but not exceeding one lakh rupees.
sub_structure.9: (9) If the Registrar has reasonable cause to believe that the company is not carrying on any business or operations, he may cause a physical verification of the registered office... and if any default is found... initiate action for the removal of the name of the company...

plain_english_summary: Startups must establish a physical registered office within 30 days of incorporation. The company's name, address, and CIN (Corporate Identity Number) must be displayed on a physical board outside the office and on all official letterheads, emails, and invoices. The Registrar can physically inspect the office.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Alteration of memorandum
text: (1) Save as provided in section 61, a company may, by a special resolution and after complying with the procedure specified in this section, alter the provisions of its memorandum.

sub_structure.2: (2) Any change in the name of a company shall be subject to the provisions of sub-sections (2) and (3) of section 4 and shall not have effect except with the approval of the Central Government in writing...
sub_structure.4: (4) The alteration of the memorandum relating to the place of the registered office from one State to another shall not have any effect unless it is approved by the Central Government...
sub_structure.8: (8) A company, which has raised money from public through prospectus and still has any unutilised amount out of the money so raised, shall not change its objects for which it raised the money through prospectus unless a special resolution is passed by the company... and the dissenting shareholders shall be given an opportunity to exit...

plain_english_summary: To change its name, business objects, or move its registered office to a different state, a startup must pass a special resolution (75% shareholder approval). Shifting states or changing the name requires explicit government approval.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Alteration of articles
text: (1) Subject to the provisions of this Act and the conditions contained in its memorandum, if any, a company may, by a special resolution, alter its articles including alterations having the effect of conversion of- (a) a private company into a public company; or (b) a public company into a private company:

sub_structure.1_proviso_1: Provided that where a company being a private company alters its articles in such a manner that they no longer include the restrictions and limitations which are required to be included in the articles of a private company under this Act, the company shall, as from the date of such alteration, cease to be a private company:
sub_structure.1_proviso_2: Provided further that any alteration having the effect of conversion of a public company into a private company shall not valid unless it its approved by an order of the Central Government...

plain_english_summary: A startup can change its internal rules (AOA) or convert from a private to a public company by passing a special resolution. However, converting from a public company back into a private company is difficult and requires Central Government approval.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Alteration of memorandum or articles to be noted in every copy
text: (1) Every alteration made in the memorandum or articles of a company shall be noted in every copy of the memorandum or articles, as the case may be.

sub_structure.2: (2) If a company makes any default in complying with the provisions of sub-section (1), the company and every officer who is in default shall be liable to a penalty of one thousand rupees for every copy of the memorandum or articles issued without such alteration.

plain_english_summary: Whenever a startup updates its MOA or AOA, it must ensure every single physical and digital copy distributed going forward includes those updates. Giving out outdated copies carries a fine of â‚¹1,000 per copy.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Rectification of name of company
text: (1) If, through inadvertence or otherwise, a company on its first registration or on its registration by a new name, is registered by a name which,- (a) in the opinion of the Central Government, is identical with or too nearly resembles the name by which a company in existence had been previously registered... it may direct the company to change its name...

sub_structure.1_b: (b) on an application by a registered proprietor of a trade mark that the name is identical with or too nearly resembles to a registered trade mark... made to the Central Government within three years of incorporation... it may direct the company to change its name...
sub_structure.3: (3) If a company is in default in complying with any direction given under sub-section (1), the Central Government shall allot a new name to the company in such manner as may be prescribed and the Registrar shall enter the new name in the register of companies...

plain_english_summary: If a startup accidentally registers a name that is too similar to an existing company or infringes on an existing trademark, the government will order the startup to change its name. If the startup ignores the order, the government will forcibly assign it a new name.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Copies of memorandum, articles, etc., to be given to members
text: (1) A company shall, on being so requested by a member, send to him within seven days of the request and subject to the payment of such fees as may be prescribed, a copy of each of the following documents, namely:- (a) the memorandum; (b) the articles; and (c) every agreement and every resolution referred to in sub-section (1) of section 117...

sub_structure.2: (2) If a company makes any default in complying with the provisions of this section, the company and every officer of the company who is in default shall be liable for each default, to a penalty of one thousand rupees for each day during which such default continues or one lakh rupees, whichever is less.

plain_english_summary: Shareholders have the right to request a copy of the company's MOA, AOA, and major resolutions. The startup must provide these within 7 days. Failing to do so triggers a daily penalty.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Conversion of companies already registered
text: (1) A company of any class registered under this Act may convert itself as a company of other class under this Act by alteration of memorandum and articles of the company in accordance with the provisions of this Chapter.

sub_structure.2: (2) Where the conversion is required to be done under this section, the Registrar shall on an application made by the company... close the former registration of the company and after registering the documents referred to in sub-section (1), issue a certificate of incorporation in the same manner as its first registration.
sub_structure.3: (3) The registration of a company under this section shall not affect any debts, liabilities, obligations or contracts incurred or entered into, by or on behalf of the company before conversion...

plain_english_summary: If a startup converts from one type of company to another (e.g., Private to Public), its past debts, contracts, and legal obligations are completely unaffected and carry over to the newly converted entity.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Subsidiary company not to hold shares in its holding company
text: (1) No company shall, either by itself or through its nominees, hold any shares in its holding company and no holding company shall allot or transfer its shares to any of its subsidiary companies and any such allotment or transfer of shares of a company to its subsidiary company shall be void:

sub_structure.1_proviso_1_c: Provided that nothing in this sub-section shall apply to a case- ... (c) where the subsidiary company is a shareholder even before it became a subsidiary company of the holding company:
sub_structure.1_proviso_2: Provided further that the subsidiary company referred to in the preceding proviso shall have a right to vote at a meeting of the holding company only in respect of the shares held by it as a legal representative or as a trustee...

plain_english_summary: A subsidiary startup cannot legally buy or hold equity shares in its parent/holding company, as this creates circular ownership. Any attempt to issue shares this way is legally void. Exceptions exist if the subsidiary acts as a trustee or acquired the shares before it became a subsidiary.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Service of documents
text: (1) A document may be served on a company or an officer thereof by sending it to the company or the officer at the registered office of the company by registered post or by speed post or by courier service or by leaving it at its registered office or by means of such electronic or other mode as may be prescribed:

sub_structure.2: (2) Save as provided in this Act or the rules made thereunder for filing of documents with the Registrar in electronic mode, a document may be served on Registrar or any member by sending it to him by post or by registered post or by speed post or by courier or by delivering at his office or address, or by such electronic or other mode as may be prescribed:

plain_english_summary: Defines the legally valid ways to send official documents or notices to a company (like a lawsuit or regulatory notice) or to its shareholders. Sending an email or using registered post to the registered office address is legally binding.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Authentication of documents, proceedings and contracts
text: Save as otherwise provided in this Act,- (a) a document or proceeding requiring authentication by a company; or (b) contracts made by or on behalf of a company, may be signed by any key managerial personnel or an officer or employee of the company duly authorised by the Board in this behalf.
plain_english_summary: A startup does not need its official 'common seal' to sign everyday contracts. Any Key Managerial Personnel (like a CEO or CFO) or a specifically board-authorized employee can legally sign documents and bind the company.

chapter_title: INCORPORATION OF COMPANY AND MATTERS INCIDENTAL THERETO
title: Execution of bills of exchange, etc.
text: (1) A bill of exchange, hundi or promissory note shall be deemed to have been made, accepted, drawn or endorsed on behalf of a company if made, accepted, drawn, or endorsed in the name of, or on behalf of or on account of, the company by any person acting under its authority, express or implied.

sub_structure.2: (2) A company may, by writing under its common seal, if any, authorise any person, either generally or in respect of any specified matters, as its attorney to execute other deeds on its behalf in any place either in or outside India:

plain_english_summary: Financial instruments like promissory notes are legally valid if signed by someone acting with the startup's authority. The company can also grant Power of Attorney to someone to execute major deeds on its behalf anywhere in the world.

title: Public offer and private placement
text: (1) A public company may issue securities- (a) to public through prospectus (herein referred to as "public offer") by complying with the provisions of this Part; (b) through private placement by complying with the provisions of Part II of this Chapter; or (c) through a rights issue or a bonus issue...

sub_structure.2: (2) A private company may issue securities- (a) by way of rights issue or bonus issue in accordance with the provisions of this Act; or (b) through private placement by complying with the provisions of Part II of this Chapter.

plain_english_summary: This is the core funding rule for startups. A private startup can ONLY raise money by offering shares directly to existing shareholders (rights/bonus issues) or to a select group of investors like VCs or angels (private placement). They are strictly forbidden from offering shares to the general public.

title: Power of Securities and Exchange Board to regulate issue and transfer of securities, etc.
text: (1) The provisions contained in this Chapter, Chapter IV and in section 127 shall,- (a) in so far as they relate to- (i) issue and transfer of securities; and (ii) non-payment of dividend, by listed companies or those companies which intend to get their securities listed... be administered by the Securities and Exchange Board...

sub_structure.1_b: (b) in any other case, be administered by the Central Government.

plain_english_summary: If a company is listed on the stock market (or planning an IPO), the rules about issuing shares are heavily regulated by SEBI. For unlisted private startups, these rules are administered by the Central Government (Ministry of Corporate Affairs).

title: Document containing offer of securities for sale to be deemed prospectus
text: (1) Where a company allots or agrees to allot any securities of the company with a view to all or any of those securities being offered for sale to the public, any document by which the offer for sale to the public is made shall, for all purposes, be deemed to be a prospectus issued by the company...
plain_english_summary: If a company gives shares to an intermediary with the intention that the intermediary will sell them to the public, the document used to advertise that sale is legally treated as a formal 'prospectus' and is subject to strict liability rules.

title: Matters to be stated in prospectus
text: (1) Every prospectus issued by or on behalf of a public company either with reference to its formation or subsequently, or by or on behalf of any person who is or has been engaged or interested in the formation of a public company, shall be dated and signed and shall state such information and set out such reports on financial information as may be specified by the Securities and Exchange Board...

sub_structure.4: (4) No prospectus shall be issued by or on behalf of a company or in relation to an intended company unless on or before the date of its publication, there has been delivered to the Registrar for registration, a copy thereof signed by every person who is named therein as a director or proposed director...
sub_structure.9: (9) If a prospectus is issued in contravention of the provisions of this section, the company shall be punishable with fine which shall not be less than fifty thousand rupees but which may extend to three lakh rupees and every person who is knowingly a party to the issue of such prospectus shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years or with fine... or with both.

plain_english_summary: A prospectus (the document used to pitch an IPO to the public) must contain specific financial information and risk reports mandated by SEBI. It must be registered with the government before publication. Violating these rules can lead to massive fines and up to 3 years in prison.

title: Variation in terms of contract or objects in prospectus
text: (1) A company shall not, at any time, vary the terms of a contract referred to in the prospectus or objects for which the prospectus was issued, except subject to the approval of, or except subject to an authority given by the company in general meeting by way of special resolution:

sub_structure.2: (2) The dissenting shareholders being those shareholders who have not agreed to the proposal to vary the terms of contracts or objects referred to in the prospectus, shall be given an exit offer by promoters or controlling shareholders at such exit price, and in such manner and conditions as may be specified by the Securities and Exchange Board...

plain_english_summary: If a startup goes public to raise funds for 'Project A', it cannot suddenly use that money for 'Project B' unless 75% of shareholders agree. The founders must also offer to buy out any shareholders who disagree with the pivot.

title: Offer of sale of shares by certain members of company
text: (1) Where certain members of a company propose, in consultation with the Board of Directors to offer, in accordance with the provisions of any law for the time being in force, whole or part of their holding of shares to the public, they may do so in accordance with such procedure as may be prescribed.

sub_structure.2: (2) Any document by which the offer of sale to the public is made shall, for all purposes, be deemed to be a prospectus issued by the company and all laws and rules made thereunder as to the contents of the prospectus and as to liability in respect of mis-statements in and omission from prospectus... shall apply...

plain_english_summary: This governs the 'Offer for Sale' (OFS) in an IPO, where early investors or founders sell their own existing shares to the public, rather than the company issuing new shares. The document used for this is strictly treated as a full prospectus.

title: Public offer of securities to be in dematerialised form
text: (1) Notwithstanding anything contained in any other provisions of this Act,- (a) every company making public offer; and (b) such other class or classes of public companies as may be prescribed, shall issue the securities only in dematerialised form by complying with the provisions of the Depositories Act, 1996 and the regulations made thereunder.

sub_structure.1A: (1A) In case of such class or classes of unlisted companies as may be prescribed, the securities shall be held or transferred only in dematerialised form in the manner laid down in the Depositories Act, 1996 and the regulations made thereunder.

plain_english_summary: Companies making a public offer (IPO) cannot issue paper stock certificates; shares must be issued in a digital, dematerialised (Demat) form. The government has also expanded this rule to mandate that large unlisted companies must keep their shares in Demat form as well.

title: Advertisement of prospectus
text: Where an advertisement of any prospectus of a company is published in any manner, it shall be necessary to specify therein the contents of its memorandum as regards the objects, the liability of members and the amount of share capital of the company, and the names of the signatories to the memorandum and the number of shares subscribed for by them, and its capital structure.
plain_english_summary: Any public advertisement for an upcoming stock offering must clearly state the company's core business objects, its capital structure, and who the original founding signatories are.

title: Shelf prospectus
text: (1) Any class or classes of companies, as the Securities and Exchange Board may provide by regulations in this behalf, may file a shelf prospectus with the Registrar at the stage of the first offer of securities included therein which shall indicate a period not exceeding one year as the period of validity of such prospectus which shall commence from the date of opening of the first offer of securities under that prospectus, and in respect of a second or subsequent offer of such securities issued during the period of validity of that prospectus, no further prospectus is required.

sub_structure.2: (2) A company filing a shelf prospectus shall be required to file an information memorandum containing all material facts relating to new charges created, changes in the financial position of the company as have occurred between the first offer of securities or the previous offer of securities and the succeeding offer of securities and such other changes as may be prescribed, with the Registrar within the prescribed time, prior to the issue of a second or subsequent offer of securities under the shelf prospectus:
sub_structure.2_proviso: Provided that where a company or any other person has received applications for the allotment of securities along with advance payments of subscription before the making of any such change, the company or other person shall intimate the changes to such applicants and if they express a desire to withdraw their application, the company or other person shall refund all the monies received as subscription within fifteen days thereof.
sub_structure.3: (3) Where an information memorandum is filed, every time an offer of securities is made under sub-section (2), such memorandum together with the shelf prospectus shall be deemed to be a prospectus.
sub_structure.explanation: Explanation. For the purposes of this section, the expression "shelf prospectus" means a prospectus in respect of which the securities or class of securities included therein are issued for subscription in one or more issues over a certain period without the issue of a further prospectus.

plain_english_summary: A 'Shelf Prospectus' allows a company to make multiple public stock offerings over a one-year period without having to file a full new prospectus every single time. They only need to file a shorter 'information memorandum' updating investors on recent financial changes.

title: Red herring prospectus
text: (1) A company proposing to make an offer of securities may issue a red herring prospectus prior to the issue of a prospectus.

sub_structure.2: (2) A company proposing to issue a red herring prospectus under sub-section (1) shall file it with the Registrar at least three days prior to the opening of the subscription list and the offer.
sub_structure.3: (3) A red herring prospectus shall carry the same obligations as are applicable to a prospectus and any variation between the red herring prospectus and a prospectus shall be highlighted as variations in the prospectus.
sub_structure.4: (4) Upon the closing of the offer of securities under this section, the prospectus stating therein the total capital raised, whether by way of debt or share capital, and the closing price of the securities and any other details as are not included in the red herring prospectus shall be filed with the Registrar and the Securities and Exchange Board.
sub_structure.explanation: Explanation. For the purposes of this section, the expression "red herring prospectus" means a prospectus which does not include complete particulars of the quantum or price of the securities included therein.

plain_english_summary: Before a final IPO price is set, a company can issue a 'Red Herring Prospectus'. This document contains all the business details but leaves out the exact number of shares being offered and their final price.

title: Issue of application forms for securities
text: (1) No form of application for the purchase of any of the securities of a company shall be issued unless such form is accompanied by an abridged prospectus:

sub_structure.1_proviso_a: Provided that nothing in this sub-section shall apply if it is shown that the form of application was issued- (a) in connection with a bona fide invitation to a person to enter into an underwriting agreement with respect to such securities; or
sub_structure.1_proviso_b: (b) in relation to securities which were not offered to the public.
sub_structure.2: (2) A copy of the prospectus shall, on a request being made by any person before the closing of the subscription list and the offer, be furnished to him.
sub_structure.3: (3) If a company makes any default in complying with the provisions of this section, it shall be liable to a penalty of fifty thousand rupees for each default.

plain_english_summary: If a company offers shares to the public, the application form must always be attached to a shortened, summary version of the prospectus (an abridged prospectus). Failing to attach it results in a â‚¹50,000 fine per default.

title: Criminal liability for mis-statements in prospectus
text: Where a prospectus, issued, circulated or distributed under this Chapter, includes any statement which is untrue or misleading in form or context in which it is included or where any inclusion or omission of any matter is likely to mislead, every person who authorises the issue of such prospectus shall be liable under section 447:

sub_structure.proviso: Provided that nothing in this section shall apply to a person if he proves that such statement or omission was immaterial or that he had reasonable grounds to believe, and did up to the time of issue of the prospectus believe, that the statement was true or the inclusion or omission was necessary.

plain_english_summary: Founders and directors face severe criminal charges (Section 447 fraud) if they knowingly include lies or omit crucial facts in a prospectus to trick the public into investing.

title: Civil liability for mis-statements in prospectus
text: (1) Where a person has subscribed for securities of a company acting on any statement included, or the inclusion or omission of any matter, in the prospectus which is misleading and has sustained any loss or damage as a consequence thereof, the company and every person who- (a) is a director of the company at the time of the issue of the prospectus; (b) has authorised himself to be named and is named in the prospectus as a director of the company... (c) is a promoter of the company; (d) has authorised the issue of the prospectus; and (e) is an expert referred to in sub-section (5) of section 26, shall, without prejudice to any punishment to which any person may be liable under section 36, be liable to pay compensation to every person who has sustained such loss or damage.

sub_structure.2_a: (2) No person shall be liable under sub-section (1), if he provesâ€” (a) that, having consented to become a director of the company, he withdrew his consent before the issue of the prospectus, and that it was issued without his authority or consent; or
sub_structure.2_b: (b) that the prospectus was issued without his knowledge or consent, and that on becoming aware of its issue, he forthwith gave a reasonable public notice that it was issued without his knowledge or consent.
sub_structure.2_c: (c) that, as regards every misleading statement purported to be made by an expert... it was a correct and fair representation of the statement... and he had reasonable ground to believe... that the person making the statement was competent to make it...
sub_structure.3: (3) Notwithstanding anything contained in this section, where it is proved that a prospectus has been issued with intent to defraud the applicants... every person referred to in sub-section (1) shall be personally responsible, without any limitation of liability, for all or any of the losses or damages that may have been incurred...

plain_english_summary: If investors lose money because they relied on a misleading prospectus, the directors, promoters, and experts who signed off on it can be sued personally to compensate the investors. If the lies were intentional fraud, the founders have unlimited personal liability.

title: Punishment for fraudulently inducing persons to invest money
text: Any person who, either knowingly or recklessly makes any statement, promise or forecast which is false, deceptive or misleading, or deliberately conceals any material facts, to induce another person to enter into, or to offer to enter into,- (a) any agreement for, or with a view to, acquiring, disposing of, subscribing for, or underwriting securities; or (b) any agreement, the purpose or the pretended purpose of which is to secure a profit to any of the parties from the yield of securities or by reference to fluctuations in the value of securities; or (c) any agreement for, or with a view to obtaining credit facilities from any bank or financial institution, shall be liable for action under section 447.
plain_english_summary: Making fake promises or reckless financial forecasts to convince an investor to buy your startup's shares, or to trick a bank into giving you a loan, is a severe corporate crime punishable as fraud.

title: Action by affected persons
text: A suit may be filed or any other action may be taken under section 34 or section 35 or section 36 by any person, group of persons or any association of persons affected by any misleading statement or the inclusion or omission of any matter in the prospectus.
plain_english_summary: Groups of investors (or shareholder associations) who were tricked by a fake prospectus can band together to file a class-action style lawsuit against the company and its founders.

title: Punishment for personation for acquisition, etc., of securities
text: (1) Any person who- (a) makes or abets making of an application in a fictitious name to a company for acquiring, or subscribing for, its securities; or (b) makes or abets making of multiple applications to a company in different names or in different combinations of his name or surname for acquiring or subscribing for its securities; or (c) otherwise induces directly or indirectly a company to allot, or register any transfer of, securities to him, or to any other person in a fictitious name, shall be liable for action under section 447.

sub_structure.2: (2) The provisions of sub-section (1) shall be prominently reproduced in every prospectus issued by a company and in every form of application for securities.
sub_structure.3: (3) Where a person has been convicted under this section, the Court may also order disgorgement of gain, if any, made by, and seizure and disposal of the securities in possession of, such person.
sub_structure.4: (4) The amount received through disgorgement or disposal of securities under sub-section (3) shall be credited to the Investor Education and Protection Fund.

plain_english_summary: It is a severe crime to use a fake name, or multiple variations of your name, to try and hoard shares during a public offering. If caught, the court will seize the illegally acquired shares and seize any profits made from them.

title: Allotment of securities by company
text: (1) No allotment of any securities of a company offered to the public for subscription shall be made unless the amount stated in the prospectus as the minimum amount has been subscribed and the sums payable on application for the amount so stated have been paid to and received by the company by cheque or other instrument.

sub_structure.2: (2) The amount payable on application on every security shall not be less than five per cent. of the nominal amount of the security or such other percentage or amount, as may be specified by the Securities and Exchange Board by making regulations in this behalf.
sub_structure.3: (3) If the stated minimum amount has not been subscribed and the sum payable on application is not received within a period of thirty days from the date of issue of the prospectus, or such other period as may be specified by the Securities and Exchange Board, the amount received under sub-section (1) shall be returned within such time and manner as may be prescribed.
sub_structure.4: (4) Whenever a company having a share capital makes any allotment of securities, it shall file with the Registrar a return of allotment in such manner as may be prescribed.
sub_structure.5: (5) In case of any default under sub-section (3) or sub-section (4), the company and its officer who is in default shall be liable to a penalty, for each default, of one thousand rupees for each day during which such default continues or one lakh rupees, whichever is less.

plain_english_summary: If a company's public offering fails to raise the minimum required amount of money within 30 days, the offering is cancelled, and all application money must be refunded to investors. Once shares are successfully allotted, the company must file a 'return of allotment' with the Registrar.

title: Short title and commencement
text: This Act may be called the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023. [cite: 27]

sub_structure.2: It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint and different dates may be appointed for different provisions of this Act... [cite: 28]

plain_english_summary: This section outlines the name of the Act and states that the government will announce when specific parts of the law officially take effect.

title: Definitions
text: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,- [cite: 34]

sub_structure.c: "Board" means the Data Protection Board of India established by the Central Government under section 18; [cite: 37]
sub_structure.i: "Data Fiduciary" means any person who alone or in conjunction with other persons determines the purpose and means of processing of personal data; [cite: 44]
sub_structure.j: "Data Principal" means the individual to whom the personal data relates... [cite: 45]
sub_structure.k: "Data Processor" means any person who processes personal data on behalf of a Data Fiduciary; [cite: 49]
sub_structure.t: "personal data" means any data about an individual who is identifiable by or in relation to such data; [cite: 72]
sub_structure.u: "personal data breach" means any unauthorised processing of personal data or accidental disclosure, acquisition, sharing, use, alteration, destruction or loss of access to personal data... [cite: 73]

plain_english_summary: This section defines the key terms used throughout the DPDP Act, such as Data Fiduciary (the entity collecting data), Data Principal (the user), and what constitutes a personal data breach.

title: Application of Act
text: Subject to the provisions of this Act, it shall apply to the processing of digital personal data within the territory of India where the personal data is collected in digital form; or in non-digital form and digitised subsequently. [cite: 83, 84, 85, 86]

sub_structure.b: also apply to processing of digital personal data outside the territory of India, if such processing is in connection with any activity related to offering of goods or services to Data Principals within the territory of India; [cite: 87]
sub_structure.c: not apply to personal data processed by an individual for any personal or domestic purpose; and personal data that is made or caused to be made publicly available... [cite: 94, 96, 98]

plain_english_summary: The DPDP Act applies to digital personal data processed inside India, and data processed outside India if it involves offering goods/services to people inside India. It does not apply to personal/domestic use or publicly available data.

title: Grounds for processing personal data
text: A person may process the personal data of a Data Principal only in accordance with the provisions of this Act and for a lawful purpose. [cite: 106]

sub_structure.1_a: for which the Data Principal has given her consent; or [cite: 107]
sub_structure.2: For the purposes of this section, the expression 'lawful purpose' means any purpose which is not expressly forbidden by law. [cite: 108]

plain_english_summary: Startups can only process a user's personal data if it is for a lawful purpose, and they either have the user's explicit consent or it falls under a specific 'legitimate use'.

title: Notice
text: Every request made to a Data Principal under section 6 for consent shall be accompanied or preceded by a notice given by the Data Fiduciary to the Data Principal... [cite: 109]

sub_structure.1_i: informing her the personal data and the purpose for which the same is proposed to be processed; [cite: 110]
sub_structure.1_ii: the manner in which she may exercise her rights under sub-section (4) of section 6 and section 13; [cite: 112]
sub_structure.1_iii: the manner in which the Data Principal may make a complaint to the Board... [cite: 113]
sub_structure.3: The Data Fiduciary shall give the Data Principal the option to access the contents of the notice referred to in sub-sections (1) and (2) in English or any language specified in the Eighth Schedule to the Constitution. [cite: 132]

plain_english_summary: Before asking for consent, companies must provide a clear notice explaining what data is being collected, why it's being collected, how the user can withdraw consent, and how to file a complaint. This notice must be available in English and other recognized Indian languages.

title: Consent
text: The consent given by the Data Principal shall be free, specific, informed, unconditional and unambiguous with a clear affirmative action, and shall signify an agreement to the processing of her personal data for the specified purpose and be limited to such personal data as is necessary for such specified purpose. [cite: 133, 134]

sub_structure.4: Where consent given by the Data Principal is the basis of processing of personal data, such Data Principal shall have the right to withdraw her consent at any time, with the ease of doing so being comparable to the ease with which such consent was given. [cite: 144]
sub_structure.6: If a Data Principal withdraws her consent... the Data Fiduciary shall, within a reasonable time, cease and cause its Data Processors to cease processing the personal data... [cite: 150]
sub_structure.7: The Data Principal may give, manage, review or withdraw her consent to the Data Fiduciary through a Consent Manager. [cite: 158]

plain_english_summary: User consent must be explicit, clear, and limited only to the data absolutely necessary for the service. Users have the right to withdraw their consent at any time just as easily as they gave it. If withdrawn, the company and its processors must stop using that data.

title: Certain legitimate uses
text: A Data Fiduciary may process personal data of a Data Principal for any of following legitimate uses... [cite: 162]

sub_structure.a: for the specified purpose for which the Data Principal has voluntarily provided her personal data to the Data Fiduciary, and in respect of which she has not indicated to the Data Fiduciary that she does not consent to the use of her personal data. [cite: 163]
sub_structure.f: for responding to a medical emergency involving a threat to the life or immediate threat to the health of the Data Principal or any other individual; [cite: 186]
sub_structure.i: for the purposes of employment or those related to safeguarding the employer from loss or liability... or provision of any service or benefit sought by a Data Principal who is an employee. [cite: 191]

plain_english_summary: Companies can process data without explicit consent in specific 'legitimate' situations, such as when a user voluntarily gives their data for a specific task, during medical emergencies, or for standard employment purposes.

title: General obligations of Data Fiduciary
text: A Data Fiduciary shall, irrespective of any agreement to the contrary or failure of a Data Principal to carry out the duties provided under this Act, be responsible for complying with the provisions of this Act... [cite: 192]

sub_structure.2: A Data Fiduciary may engage, appoint, use or otherwise involve a Data Processor to process personal data on its behalf for any activity related to offering of goods or services to Data Principals only under a valid contract. [cite: 193]
sub_structure.5: A Data Fiduciary shall protect personal data in its possession or under its control... by taking reasonable security safeguards to prevent personal data breach. [cite: 198]
sub_structure.6: In the event of a personal data breach, the Data Fiduciary shall give the Board and each affected Data Principal, intimation of such breach in such form and manner as may be prescribed. [cite: 199]
sub_structure.7: A Data Fiduciary shall, unless retention is necessary for compliance with any law for the time being in force, erase personal data, upon the Data Principal withdrawing her consent or as soon as it is reasonable to assume that the specified purpose is no longer being served... [cite: 200, 201, 208]
sub_structure.10: A Data Fiduciary shall establish an effective mechanism to redress the grievances of Data Principals. [cite: 223]

plain_english_summary: Data Fiduciaries must ensure data accuracy, implement security safeguards, and use valid contracts with third-party processors. They must report data breaches to both the government Board and affected users. They must also delete data when it's no longer needed or consent is withdrawn, and maintain a grievance redressal system.

title: Processing of personal data of children
text: The Data Fiduciary shall, before processing any personal data of a child or a person with disability who has a lawful guardian obtain verifiable consent of the parent of such child or the lawful guardian... [cite: 225]

sub_structure.2: A Data Fiduciary shall not undertake such processing of personal data that is likely to cause any detrimental effect on the well-being of a child. [cite: 227]
sub_structure.3: A Data Fiduciary shall not undertake tracking or behavioural monitoring of children or targeted advertising directed at children. [cite: 228]

plain_english_summary: To process a child's data (under 18), companies must get verifiable consent from a parent or guardian. Companies are strictly prohibited from tracking children, directing targeted ads at them, or processing data in a way that could harm them.

title: Additional obligations of Significant Data Fiduciary
text: The Central Government may notify any Data Fiduciary or class of Data Fiduciaries as Significant Data Fiduciary, on the basis of an assessment of such relevant factors as it may determine... [cite: 231]

sub_structure.1_factors: Factors include volume and sensitivity of personal data processed; risk to the rights of Data Principal; potential impact on the sovereignty and integrity of India... [cite: 234, 235, 236]
sub_structure.2_a: appoint a Data Protection Officer who shall represent the Significant Data Fiduciary... be based in India; be an individual responsible to the Board of Directors... [cite: 241, 244, 245, 246]
sub_structure.2_b: appoint an independent data auditor to carry out data audit... [cite: 248]
sub_structure.2_c: undertake... periodic Data Protection Impact Assessment... [cite: 250, 251]

plain_english_summary: If the government classifies a company as a 'Significant Data Fiduciary' (usually due to high data volume or sensitive operations), the company must meet stricter rules. This includes appointing a Data Protection Officer based in India, hiring an independent data auditor, and conducting regular Data Protection Impact Assessments.

chapter_title: RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF DATA PRINCIPAL
title: Right to access information about personal data
text: The Data Principal shall have the right to obtain from the Data Fiduciary to whom she has previously given consent... a summary of personal data which is being processed by such Data Fiduciary and the processing activities undertaken by that Data Fiduciary with respect to such personal data; [cite: 256, 257]

sub_structure.1_b: the identities of all other Data Fiduciaries and Data Processors with whom the personal data has been shared by such Data Fiduciary, along with a description of the personal data so shared; [cite: 258]
sub_structure.1_c: any other information related to the personal data of such Data Principal and its processing, as may be prescribed. [cite: 260]
sub_structure.2: Nothing contained in clause (b) or clause (c) of sub-section (1) shall apply in respect of the sharing of any personal data by the said Data Fiduciary with any other Data Fiduciary authorised by law to obtain such personal data... [cite: 261]

plain_english_summary: Users have the right to ask a company for a summary of the personal data it holds on them, what it is doing with that data, and the identities of any third parties the data has been shared with. [cite: 256, 257, 258]

chapter_title: RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF DATA PRINCIPAL
title: Right to correction and erasure of personal data
text: A Data Principal shall have the right to correction, completion, updating and erasure of her personal data for the processing of which she has previously given consent... [cite: 271]

sub_structure.2: A Data Fiduciary shall, upon receiving a request for correction, completion or updating from a Data Principal, (a) correct the inaccurate or misleading personal data; (b) complete the incomplete personal data; and (c) update the personal data. [cite: 272, 273, 274, 275]
sub_structure.3: A Data Principal shall make a request in such manner as may be prescribed to the Data Fiduciary for erasure of her personal data, and upon receipt of such a request, the Data Fiduciary shall erase her personal data unless retention of the same is necessary for the specified purpose or for compliance with any law for the time being in force. [cite: 276]

plain_english_summary: Users have the right to ask a startup to correct, update, complete, or completely delete their personal data. The company must comply unless it legally needs to retain the data for compliance reasons. [cite: 271, 272, 276]

chapter_title: RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF DATA PRINCIPAL
title: Right of grievance redressal
text: A Data Principal shall have the right to have readily available means of grievance redressal provided by a Data Fiduciary or Consent Manager in respect of any act or omission of such Data Fiduciary or Consent Manager regarding the performance of its obligations... [cite: 277]

sub_structure.2: The Data Fiduciary or Consent Manager shall respond to any grievances referred to in sub-section (1) within such period as may be prescribed... [cite: 278]
sub_structure.3: The Data Principal shall exhaust the opportunity of redressing her grievance under this section before approaching the Board. [cite: 279]

plain_english_summary: Users have the right to an easy-to-use grievance redressal mechanism provided by the company. Users must try to resolve their complaints directly with the company before escalating the issue to the Data Protection Board. [cite: 277, 279]

chapter_title: RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF DATA PRINCIPAL
title: Right to nominate
text: A Data Principal shall have the right to nominate, in such manner as may be prescribed, any other individual, who shall, in the event of death or incapacity of the Data Principal, exercise the rights of the Data Principal in accordance with the provisions of this Act... [cite: 280]

sub_structure.2: For the purposes of this section, the expression "incapacity" means inability to exercise the rights of the Data Principal under the provisions of this Act or the rules made thereunder due to unsoundness of mind or infirmity of body. [cite: 281]

plain_english_summary: A user can nominate another person to exercise their data rights (like requesting data deletion) on their behalf if the user dies or becomes physically or mentally incapacitated. [cite: 280, 281]

chapter_title: RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF DATA PRINCIPAL
title: Duties of Data Principal
text: A Data Principal shall perform the following duties, namely:- (a) comply with the provisions of all applicable laws for the time being in force while exercising rights under the provisions of this Act; [cite: 282, 283]

sub_structure.b: to ensure not to impersonate another person while providing her personal data for a specified purpose; [cite: 284]
sub_structure.c: to ensure not to suppress any material information while providing her personal data for any document, unique identifier, proof of identity or proof of address issued by the State or any of its instrumentalities; [cite: 285]
sub_structure.d: to ensure not to register a false or frivolous grievance or complaint with a Data Fiduciary or the Board; and [cite: 286, 287]
sub_structure.e: to furnish only such information as is verifiably authentic, while exercising the right to correction or erasure under the provisions of this Act or the rules made thereunder. [cite: 288]

plain_english_summary: Users also have legal duties. They must not impersonate others, submit fake documents, file fake or frivolous complaints, or provide fake information when asking for data corrections. Breaking these rules can lead to fines for the user. [cite: 282, 284, 285, 286, 288]

title: Processing of personal data outside India
text: The Central Government may, by notification, restrict the transfer of personal data by a Data Fiduciary for processing to such country or territory outside India as may be so notified. [cite: 295]

sub_structure.2: Nothing contained in this section shall restrict the applicability of any law for the time being in force in India that provides for a higher degree of protection for or restriction on transfer of personal data by a Data Fiduciary outside India... [cite: 296]

plain_english_summary: The Indian government has the power to create a 'blacklist' of countries where Indian startups and companies are strictly forbidden from transferring user data for processing. Existing laws with stricter data transfer rules still apply. [cite: 295, 296]

title: Exemptions
text: The provisions of Chapter II, except sub-sections (1) and (5) of section 8, and those of Chapter III and section 16 shall not apply where... processing of personal data is necessary for enforcing any legal right or claim; [cite: 298, 299, 300, 301]

sub_structure.1_b: the processing of personal data by any court or tribunal or any other body in India which is entrusted by law with the performance of any judicial or quasi-judicial or regulatory or supervisory function... [cite: 302]
sub_structure.1_c: personal data is processed in the interest of prevention, detection, investigation or prosecution of any offence or contravention of any law... [cite: 303]
sub_structure.2_b: necessary for research, archiving or statistical purposes if the personal data is not to be used to take any decision specific to a Data Principal... [cite: 329]
sub_structure.3: The Central Government may... notify certain Data Fiduciaries or class of Data Fiduciaries, including startups, as Data Fiduciaries to whom the provisions of section 5, sub-sections (3) and (7) of section 8 and sections 10 and 11 shall not apply. [cite: 330]

plain_english_summary: Many user rights and company obligations do not apply in specific scenarios, such as legal investigations, court orders, or statistical research. Crucially for founders, the government can exempt notified 'startups' from having to provide data notices, retain data accuracy, or grant user access rights. [cite: 298, 303, 329, 330]

title: Establishment of Board
text: With effect from such date as the Central Government may, by notification, appoint, there shall be established, for the purposes of this Act, a Board to be called the Data Protection Board of India. [cite: 336]

sub_structure.2: The Board shall be a body corporate by the name aforesaid, having perpetual succession and a common seal, with power, subject to the provisions of this Act, to acquire, hold and dispose of property... [cite: 337]
sub_structure.3: The headquarters of the Board shall be at such place as the Central Government may notify. [cite: 338]

plain_english_summary: This section officially establishes the Data Protection Board of India, which will function as an independent corporate body to enforce the Act. [cite: 336, 337]

title: Composition and qualifications for appointment of Chairperson and Members
text: The Board shall consist of a Chairperson and such number of other Members as the Central Government may notify. [cite: 339]

sub_structure.2: The Chairperson and other Members shall be appointed by the Central Government in such manner as may be prescribed. [cite: 340]
sub_structure.3: The Chairperson and other Members shall be a person of ability, integrity and standing who possesses special knowledge or practical experience in the fields of data governance, administration or implementation of laws related to social or consumer protection, dispute resolution... and at least one among them shall be an expert in the field of law. [cite: 341]

plain_english_summary: The Data Protection Board will be led by a Chairperson and other members chosen by the government. These individuals must be experts in areas like data governance, law, IT, or consumer protection. [cite: 339, 340, 341]

title: Salary, allowances payable to and term of office
text: The salary, allowances and other terms and conditions of service of the Chairperson and other Members shall be such as may be prescribed, and shall not be varied to their disadvantage after their appointment. [cite: 342]

sub_structure.2: The Chairperson and other Members shall hold office for a term of two years and shall be eligible for re-appointment. [cite: 343]

plain_english_summary: The terms of service for the Board members will be defined by the government. Members will serve two-year terms and can be re-appointed. [cite: 342, 343]

sub_structure.c: to ensure not to suppress any material information while providing her personal data for any document, unique identifier, proof of identity or proof of address issued by the State or any of its instrumentalities; [cite: 285]
sub_structure.d: to ensure not to register a false or frivolous grievance or complaint with a Data Fiduciary or the Board; and [cite: 286, 287]
sub_structure.e: to furnish only such information as is verifiably authentic, while exercising the right to correction or erasure under the provisions of this Act or the rules made thereunder. [cite: 288]

plain_english_summary: Users also have legal duties. They must not impersonate others, submit fake documents, file fake or frivolous complaints, or provide fake information when asking for data corrections. Breaking these rules can lead to fines for the user. [cite: 282, 284, 285, 286, 288]

title: Processing of personal data outside India
text: The Central Government may, by notification, restrict the transfer of personal data by a Data Fiduciary for processing to such country or territory outside India as may be so notified. [cite: 295]

sub_structure.2: Nothing contained in this section shall restrict the applicability of any law for the time being in force in India that provides for a higher degree of protection for or restriction on transfer of personal data by a Data Fiduciary outside India... [cite: 296]

plain_english_summary: The Indian government has the power to create a 'blacklist' of countries where Indian startups and companies are strictly forbidden from transferring user data for processing. Existing laws with stricter data transfer rules still apply. [cite: 295, 296]

title: Exemptions
text: The provisions of Chapter II, except sub-sections (1) and (5) of section 8, and those of Chapter III and section 16 shall not apply where... processing of personal data is necessary for enforcing any legal right or claim; [cite: 298, 299, 300, 301]

sub_structure.1_b: the processing of personal data by any court or tribunal or any other body in India which is entrusted by law with the performance of any judicial or quasi-judicial or regulatory or supervisory function... [cite: 302]
sub_structure.1_c: personal data is processed in the interest of prevention, detection, investigation or prosecution of any offence or contravention of any law... [cite: 303]
sub_structure.2_b: necessary for research, archiving or statistical purposes if the personal data is not to be used to take any decision specific to a Data Principal... [cite: 329]
sub_structure.3: The Central Government may... notify certain Data Fiduciaries or class of Data Fiduciaries, including startups, as Data Fiduciaries to whom the provisions of section 5, sub-sections (3) and (7) of section 8 and sections 10 and 11 shall not apply. [cite: 330]

plain_english_summary: Many user rights and company obligations do not apply in specific scenarios, such as legal investigations, court orders, or statistical research. Crucially for founders, the government can exempt notified 'startups' from having to provide data notices, retain data accuracy, or grant user access rights. [cite: 298, 303, 329, 330]

title: Establishment of Board
text: With effect from such date as the Central Government may, by notification, appoint, there shall be established, for the purposes of this Act, a Board to be called the Data Protection Board of India. [cite: 336]

sub_structure.2: The Board shall be a body corporate by the name aforesaid, having perpetual succession and a common seal, with power, subject to the provisions of this Act, to acquire, hold and dispose of property... [cite: 337]
sub_structure.3: The headquarters of the Board shall be at such place as the Central Government may notify. [cite: 338]

plain_english_summary: This section officially establishes the Data Protection Board of India, which will function as an independent corporate body to enforce the Act. [cite: 336, 337]

title: Composition and qualifications for appointment of Chairperson and Members
text: The Board shall consist of a Chairperson and such number of other Members as the Central Government may notify. [cite: 339]

sub_structure.2: The Chairperson and other Members shall be appointed by the Central Government in such manner as may be prescribed. [cite: 340]
sub_structure.3: The Chairperson and other Members shall be a person of ability, integrity and standing who possesses special knowledge or practical experience in the fields of data governance, administration or implementation of laws related to social or consumer protection, dispute resolution... and at least one among them shall be an expert in the field of law. [cite: 341]

plain_english_summary: The Data Protection Board will be led by a Chairperson and other members chosen by the government. These individuals must be experts in areas like data governance, law, IT, or consumer protection. [cite: 339, 340, 341]

title: Salary, allowances payable to and term of office
text: The salary, allowances and other terms and conditions of service of the Chairperson and other Members shall be such as may be prescribed, and shall not be varied to their disadvantage after their appointment. [cite: 342]

sub_structure.2: The Chairperson and other Members shall hold office for a term of two years and shall be eligible for re-appointment. [cite: 343]

plain_english_summary: The terms of service for the Board members will be defined by the government. Members will serve two-year terms and can be re-appointed. [cite: 342, 343]

title: Alternate dispute resolution
text: If the Board is of the opinion that any complaint may be resolved by mediation, it may direct the parties concerned to attempt resolution of the dispute through such mediation by such mediator as the parties may mutually agree upon, or as provided for under any law for the time being in force in India. [cite: 441]
plain_english_summary: The Board can direct startups and users to resolve their complaints through mediation if it believes the dispute can be settled that way. [cite: 441]

title: Voluntary undertaking
text: The Board may accept a voluntary undertaking in respect of any matter related to observance of the provisions of this Act from any person at any stage of a proceeding under section 28. [cite: 442]

sub_structure.2: The voluntary undertaking referred to in sub-section (1) may include an undertaking to take such action within such time as may be determined by the Board, or refrain from taking such action, and or publicising such undertaking. [cite: 443]
sub_structure.4: The acceptance of the voluntary undertaking by the Board shall constitute a bar on proceedings under the provisions of this Act as regards the contents of the voluntary undertaking, except in cases covered by sub-section (5). [cite: 445]
sub_structure.5: Where a person fails to adhere to any term of the voluntary undertaking accepted by the Board, such breach shall be deemed to be breach of the provisions of this Act and the Board may... proceed in accordance with the provisions of section 33. [cite: 446]

plain_english_summary: A startup can offer a 'voluntary undertaking' to the Board, promising to fix a compliance issue or take specific actions. If accepted, this can pause proceedings, but breaking the promise counts as a breach of the Act. [cite: 442, 443, 445, 446]

title: Penalties
text: If the Board determines on conclusion of an inquiry that breach of the provisions of this Act or the rules made thereunder by a person is significant, it may, after giving the person an opportunity of being heard, impose such monetary penalty specified in the Schedule. [cite: 449, 453]

sub_structure.2_factors: While determining the amount of monetary penalty... the Board shall have regard to the following matters, namely:- (a) the nature, gravity and duration of the breach; (b) the type and nature of the personal data affected... (e) whether the person took any action to mitigate the effects and consequences of the breach... [cite: 454, 455, 456, 459]

plain_english_summary: The Board has the power to impose significant monetary penalties (fines) on companies if an inquiry concludes they broke the law. Factors like the gravity of the breach and actions taken to mitigate it are considered. [cite: 449, 453, 454, 455, 459]

title: Crediting sums realised by way of penalties to Consolidated Fund of India
text: All sums realised by way of penalties imposed by the Board under this Act, shall be credited to the Consolidated Fund of India. [cite: 463]
plain_english_summary: Any money collected from fines and penalties under this Act goes directly into the Consolidated Fund of India. [cite: 463]

title: Protection of action taken in good faith
text: No suit, prosecution or other legal proceedings shall lie against the Central Government, the Board, its Chairperson and any Member, officer or employee thereof for anything which is done or intended to be done in good faith under the provisions of this Act or the rules made thereunder. [cite: 467]
plain_english_summary: Government officials and Board members are legally protected from lawsuits for actions they take in good faith while enforcing this Act. [cite: 467]

title: Power to call for information
text: The Central Government may, for the purposes of this Act, require the Board and any Data Fiduciary or intermediary to furnish such information as it may call for. [cite: 468]
plain_english_summary: The Central Government can demand information from the Board, Data Fiduciaries (startups), or intermediaries for the purposes of administering the Act. [cite: 468]

title: Power of Central Government to issue directions
text: The Central Government or any of its officers specially authorised by it in this behalf may, upon receipt of a reference in writing from the Board... direct any agency of the Central Government or any intermediary to block for access by the public or cause to be blocked for access by the public any such information. [cite: 469, 472]

sub_structure.1_a: intimates the imposition of monetary penalty by the Board on a Data Fiduciary in two or more instances; and [cite: 470]
sub_structure.1_b: advises, in the interests of the general public, the blocking for access by the public to any information generated, transmitted, received, stored or hosted, in any computer resource that enables such Data Fiduciary to carry on any activity relating to offering of goods or services... [cite: 471]
sub_structure.2: Every intermediary who receives a direction issued under sub-section (1) shall be bound to comply with the same. [cite: 473]

plain_english_summary: If a company repeatedly violates the Act and is penalized multiple times, the Central Government can order intermediaries to block public access to the company's website or app in the interest of the general public. [cite: 470, 471, 472]

title: Consistency with other laws
text: The provisions of this Act shall be in addition to and not in derogation of any other law for the time being in force. [cite: 484] In the event of any conflict between a provision of this Act and a provision of any other law for the time being in force, the provision of this Act shall prevail to the extent of such conflict. [cite: 485]
plain_english_summary: This law applies alongside other existing Indian laws. However, if there is a conflict between this Act and another law, this Act's rules will override the conflicting parts. [cite: 484, 485]

title: Bar of jurisdiction
text: No civil court shall have the jurisdiction to entertain any suit or proceeding in respect of any matter for which the Board is empowered under the provisions of this Act and no injunction shall be granted by any court or other authority in respect of any action taken or to be taken in pursuance of any power under the provisions of this Act. [cite: 486]
plain_english_summary: Regular civil courts are not allowed to handle cases or grant injunctions on matters that are specifically under the authority of the Data Protection Board. [cite: 486]

title: Power to make rules
text: The Central Government may, by notification, and subject to the condition of previous publication, make rules not inconsistent with the provisions of this Act, to carry out the purposes of this Act. [cite: 487]

sub_structure.2_a: the manner in which the notice given by the Data Fiduciary to a Data Principal shall inform her, under sub-section (1) of section 5; [cite: 489]
sub_structure.2_c: the manner of accountability and the obligations of Consent Manager under sub-section (8) of section 6; [cite: 491]
sub_structure.2_f: the form and manner of intimation of personal data breach to the Board under sub-section (6) of section 8; [cite: 494]

plain_english_summary: The Central Government has the authority to create and publish specific rules to implement the provisions and purposes of the DPDP Act. [cite: 487, 488]

title: Short title, extent and commencement
text: (1) This Act may be called the Copyright Act, 1957.

sub_structure.2: (2) It extends to the whole of India.
sub_structure.3: (3) It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint.

plain_english_summary: Establishes the official name of the Act and confirms its jurisdiction over the entirety of India.

title: Interpretation
text: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,-
