sub_structure.ff: "communication to the public" means making any work or performance available for being seen or heard or otherwise enjoyed by the public directly or by any means of display or diffusion...
sub_structure.m: "infringing copy" means- (i) in relation to a literary, dramatic, musical or artistic work, a reproduction thereof otherwise than in the form of a cinematograph film... if such reproduction, copy or sound recording is made or imported in contravention of the provisions of this Act;
sub_structure.o: "literary work" includes computer programmes, tables and compilations including computer databases;

plain_english_summary: Defines key terms used in the Act. Crucially for tech startups, it classifies 'computer programmes' and 'computer databases' as literary works, thereby granting them copyright protection.

title: Meaning of publication
text: For the purposes of this Act, "publication" means making a work available to the public by issue of copies or by communicating the work to the public.
plain_english_summary: Defines publication as issuing physical copies or making a work digitally available/communicated to the public.

title: When work not deemed to be published or performed in public
text: Except in relation to infringement of copyright, a work shall not be deemed to be published or performed in public, if published, or performed in public, without the licence of the owner of the copyright.
plain_english_summary: If someone publishes or performs a work without the permission of the copyright owner, it does not legally count as an official 'publication', except when considering it an infringement.

title: When work deemed to be first published in India
text: For the purposes of this Act, a work published in India shall be deemed to be first published in India, notwithstanding that it has been published simultaneously in some other country, unless such other country provides a shorter term of copyright for such work; and a work shall be deemed to be published simultaneously in India and in another country if the time between the publication in India and the publication in such other country does not exceed thirty days...
plain_english_summary: If an app or software is released in India and another country within 30 days of each other, it is legally considered to be 'first published' in India for the purposes of local copyright protection.

title: Certain disputes to be decided by Appellate Board
text: If any question arises- (a) whether a work has been published or as to the date on which a work was published... or (b) whether the term of copyright for any work is shorter in any other country... it shall be referred to the Appellate Board constituted under section 11 whose decision thereon shall be final:

sub_structure.proviso: Provided that if in the opinion of the Appellate Board, the issue of copies or communication to the public referred to in section 3 was of an insignificant nature, it shall not be deemed to be publication for the purposes of that section.

plain_english_summary: If there is a dispute about whether a work was actually published, or when it was published, the Appellate Board makes the final decision.

title: Nationality of author where the making of unpublished work is extended over considerable period
text: Where, in the case of an unpublished work, the making of the work is extended over a considerable period, the author of the work shall, for the purposes of this Act, be deemed to be a citizen of, or domiciled in, that country of which he was a citizen or wherein he was domiciled during any substantial part of that period.
plain_english_summary: If an unpublished work takes a long time to create, the author's nationality is determined by where they were a citizen or lived for the majority of that creation period.

title: Domicile of corporations
text: For the purposes of this Act, a body corporate shall be deemed to be domiciled in India if it is incorporated under any law in force in India.
plain_english_summary: A company or startup is considered to be 'domiciled' (resident) in India if it is officially incorporated under Indian law.

title: Copyright Office
text: (1) There shall be established for the purposes of this Act an office to be called the Copyright Office.

sub_structure.2: (2) The Copyright Office shall be under the immediate control of the Registrar of Copyrights who shall act under the superintendence and direction of the Central Government.
sub_structure.3: (3) There shall be a seal for the Copyright Office.

plain_english_summary: Establishes a dedicated Copyright Office overseen by the Registrar of Copyrights and directed by the Central Government.

title: Registrar and Deputy Registrars of Copyrights
text: (1) The Central Government shall appoint a Registrar of Copyrights and may appoint one or more Deputy Registrars of Copyrights.

sub_structure.2: (2) A Deputy Registrar of Copyrights shall discharge under the superintendence and direction of the Registrar of Copyrights such functions of the Registrar under this Act as the Registrar may, from time to time, assign to him...

plain_english_summary: The Central Government appoints the Registrar of Copyrights and Deputy Registrars who manage the copyright registration and dispute procedures.

title: Appellate Board
text: (1) The Appellate Board established under section 83 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999 (47 of 1999) shall, on and from the commencement of Part XIV of Chapter VI of the Finance Act, 2017, be the Appellate Board for the purposes of this Act and the said Appellate Board shall exercise the jurisdiction, powers and authority conferred on it by or under this Act.
plain_english_summary: The same Appellate Board used for Trademarks acts as the Appellate Board for Copyright issues, handling higher-level disputes.

title: Powers and procedure of Appellate Board
text: (1) The Appellate Board shall, subject to any rules that may be made under this Act, have power to regulate its own procedure, including the fixing of places and times of its sittings:

sub_structure.7: (7) The Appellate Board shall be deemed to be a civil court for the purposes of sections 345 and 346 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (2 of 1974) and all proceedings before the Board shall be deemed to be judicial proceedings...

plain_english_summary: Outlines the powers of the Appellate Board, giving it the authority of a civil court and designating its hearings as official judicial proceedings.

title: Works in which copyright subsists
text: (1) Subject to the provisions of this section and the other provisions of this Act, copyright shall subsist throughout India in the following classes of works, that is to say,- (a) original literary, dramatic, musical and artistic works; (b) cinematograph films; and (c) sound recording.

sub_structure.2: (2) Copyright shall not subsist in any work specified in sub-section (1)... unless- (i) in the case of a published work, the work is first published in India, or where the work is first published outside India, the author is at the date of such publication... a citizen of India;
sub_structure.3: (3) Copyright shall not subsist- (a) in any cinematograph film if a substantial part of the film is an infringement of the copyright in any other work;

plain_english_summary: Identifies exactly what can be copyrighted: original literary/dramatic/artistic works (which includes software), movies, and sound recordings. It excludes works that heavily infringe on other existing copyrighted material.

title: Meaning of copyright
text: For the purposes of this Act, "copyright" means the exclusive right subject to the provisions of this Act, to do or authorise the doing of any of the following acts in respect of a work or any substantial part thereof, namely-

sub_structure.a: (a) in the case of a literary, dramatic or musical work, not being a computer programme,- (i) to reproduce the work in any material form including the storing of it in any medium by electronic means; (ii) to issue copies of the work to the public... (vi) to make any adaptation of the work;
sub_structure.b: (b) in the case of a computer programme: (i) to do any of the acts specified in clause (a); (ii) to sell or give on commercial rental or offer for sale or for commercial rental any copy of the computer programmer...

plain_english_summary: Defines exactly what a copyright owner is allowed to do. For software/code (computer programmes), it includes the exclusive right to reproduce it, adapt it, sell it, or rent it commercially.

title: Special provision regarding Copyright in designs registered or Capable of being registered under the Designs Act, 2000
text: (1) Copyright shall not subsist under this Act in any design which is registered under the Designs Act, 2000 (16 of 2000).

sub_structure.2: (2) Copyright in any design, which is capable of being registered under the Designs Act, 2000 (16 of 2000) but which has not been so registered, shall cease as soon as any article to which the design has been applied has been reproduced more than fifty times by an industrial process...

plain_english_summary: If you register a product's physical design under the Designs Act, it loses its protection under the Copyright Act. If you don't register it, its copyright automatically vanishes after the product is manufactured industrially more than 50 times.

title: No copyright except as provided in this Act
text: No person shall be entitled to copyright or any similar right in any work, whether published or unpublished, otherwise than under and in accordance with the provisions of this Act or of any other law for the time being in force, but nothing in this section shall be construed as abrogating any right or jurisdiction to restrain a breach of trust or confidence.
plain_english_summary: There are no common law copyrights in India; all copyright claims must stem directly from this Act. However, NDAs and breach of confidence claims (like stealing a startup idea) are still separate, valid legal actions.

chapter_title: OWNERSHIP OF COPYRIGHT AND THE RIGHTS OF THE OWNER
title: First owner of copyright
text: Subject to the provisions of this Act, the author of a work shall be the first owner of the copyright therein:

sub_structure.c: (c) in the case of a work made in the course of the author's employment under a contract of service or apprenticeship... the employer shall, in the absence of any agreement to the contrary, be the first owner of the copyright therein;

plain_english_summary: Normally, the creator of a work owns the copyright. However, for startups, if an employee writes code or creates content as part of their job (under a contract of service), the company automatically owns the copyright, unless an agreement says otherwise.

chapter_title: OWNERSHIP OF COPYRIGHT AND THE RIGHTS OF THE OWNER
title: Assignment of copyright
text: (1) The owner of the copyright in an existing work or the prospective owner of the copyright in a future work may assign to any person the copyright either wholly or partially and either generally or subject to limitations and either for the whole term of the copyright or any part thereof:

sub_structure.proviso_1: Provided that in the case of the assignment of copyright in any future work, the assignment shall take effect only when the work comes into existence.
sub_structure.proviso_2: Provided further that no such assignment shall be applied to any medium or mode of exploitation of the work which did not exit or was not in commercial use at the time when the assignment was made...

plain_english_summary: A copyright owner can transfer or sell (assign) their rights entirely or partially. Crucially, if you assign rights today, that assignment doesn't cover new technologies or mediums that haven't been invented yet, unless explicitly stated.

chapter_title: OWNERSHIP OF COPYRIGHT AND THE RIGHTS OF THE OWNER
title: Mode of assignment
text: (1) No assignment of the copyright in any work shall be valid unless it is in writing signed by the assignor or by his duly authorised agent.

sub_structure.2: (2) The assignment of copyright in any work shall identify such work, and shall specify the rights assigned and the duration and territorial extent of such assignment.
sub_structure.5: (5) If the period of assignment is not stated, it shall be deemed to be five years from the date of assignment.
sub_structure.6: (6) If the territorial extent of assignment of the rights is not specified, it shall be presumed to extend within India.

plain_english_summary: All copyright transfers must be in writing. If a startup acquires copyright but forgets to specify the time limit or geography in the contract, the law automatically defaults the transfer to just 5 years and limits it only to India.

chapter_title: OWNERSHIP OF COPYRIGHT AND THE RIGHTS OF THE OWNER
title: Disputes with respect to assignment of copyright
text: (1) If an assignee fails to make sufficient exercise of the rights assigned to him, and such failure is not attributable to any act or omission of the assignor, then, the Appellate Board may, on receipt of a complaint from the assignor and after holding such inquiry as it may deem necessary, revoke such assignment.

sub_structure.2: (2) If any dispute arises with respect to the assignment of any copyright, the Appellate Board may... pass such order as it may deem fit including an order for the recovery of any royalty payable:

plain_english_summary: If someone buys the rights to your work but never uses or publishes it, the Appellate Board can revoke the transfer and give the rights back to the original creator.

title: Short title, extent, commencement and application
text: (1) This Act may be called the Information Technology Act, 2000.

sub_structure.2: (2) It shall extend to the whole of India and, save as otherwise provided in this Act, it applies also to any offence or contravention thereunder committed outside India by any person.

plain_english_summary: This establishes the IT Act, noting that it has extraterritorial reachâ€”meaning it can penalize someone for hacking or cybercrimes committed against Indian systems even if they are physically outside India.

title: Definitions
text: (1) In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,-

sub_structure.a: (a) "access" with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions means gaining entry into, instructing or communicating with the logical, arithmetical, or memory function resources of a computer, computer system or computer network;
sub_structure.t: (t) "electronic record" means data, record or data generated, image or sound stored, received or sent in an electronic form or micro film or computer generated micro fiche;
sub_structure.w: (w) "intermediary", with respect to any particular electronic records, means any person who on behalf of another person receives, stores or transmits that record or provides any service with respect to that record and includes telecom service providers, network service providers, internet service providers, web-hosting service providers...

plain_english_summary: Defines core technical terms. For startups, the most important definition is 'intermediary', which classifies SaaS companies, cloud hosts, and search engines as entities that store or handle data on behalf of others.

title: Authentication of electronic records
text: (1) Subject to the provisions of this section any subscriber may authenticate an electronic record by affixing his digital signature.

sub_structure.2: (2) The authentication of the electronic record shall be effected by the use of asymmetric crypto system and hash function which envelop and transform the initial electronic record into another electronic record.

plain_english_summary: Legalizes the use of digital signatures to authenticate digital documents, specifically validating the use of asymmetric cryptography and hash functions.

title: Electronic signature
text: (1) Notwithstanding anything contained in section 3, but subject to the provisions of sub-section (2), a subscriber may authenticate any electronic record by such electronic signature or electronic authentication technique which- (a) is considered reliable; and (b) may be specified in the Second Schedule.
plain_english_summary: Expands beyond traditional 'digital signatures' to allow alternative forms of 'electronic signatures', provided the technique is reliable and officially recognized by the government.

title: Legal recognition of electronic records
text: Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be in writing or in the typewritten or printed form, then, notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such information or matter is- (a) rendered or made available in an electronic form; and (b) accessible so as to be usable for a subsequent reference.
plain_english_summary: This is a foundational rule: if any old law requires a document to be in physical 'writing', a digital or electronic document is legally equivalent and acceptable, as long as it can be accessed later.

title: Legal recognition of electronic signatures
text: Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be authenticated by affixing the signature or any document shall be signed or bear the signature of any person, then, notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied, if such information or matter is authenticated by means of electronic signature affixed in such manner as may be prescribed by the Central Government.
plain_english_summary: If any law requires a physical signature, a valid electronic signature carries the exact same legal weight.

title: Use of electronic records and electronic signatures in Government and its agencies
text: (1) Where any law provides for- (a) the filing of any form, application or any other document with any office, authority, body or agency owned or controlled by the appropriate Government in a particular manner; (b) the issue or grant of any licence, permit, sanction or approval by whatever name called in a particular manner; (c) the receipt or payment of money in a particular manner, then, notwithstanding anything contained in any other law for the time being in force, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such filing, issue, grant, receipt or payment, as the case may be, is effected by means of such electronic form as may be prescribed by the appropriate Government.
plain_english_summary: Allows citizens and companies to file forms, get licenses, and make payments to the government electronically, rather than using paper systems.

title: Delivery of services by service provider
text: (1) The appropriate Government may, for the purposes of this Chapter and for efficient delivery of services to the public through electronic means authorise, by order, any service provider to set up, maintain and upgrade the computerised facilities and perform such other services as it may specify, by notification in the Official Gazette.

sub_structure.2: (2) The appropriate Government may also authorise any service provider authorised under sub-section (1) to collect, retain and appropriate such service charges, as may be prescribed by the appropriate Government for the purpose of providing such services, from the person availing such service.

plain_english_summary: The government can hire private companies (service providers) to deliver digital public services and allows those companies to collect a service charge from users.

title: Retention of electronic records
text: (1) Where any law provides that documents, records or information shall be retained for any specific period, then, that requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such documents, records or information are retained in the electronic form, if- (a) the information contained therein remains accessible so as to be usable for a subsequent reference: (b) the electronic record is retained in the format in which it was originally generated, sent or received... (c) the details which will facilitate the identification of the origin, destination, date and time of despatch or receipt of such electronic record are available in the electronic record...
plain_english_summary: If the law requires you to keep a document for a certain number of years, keeping a digital copy counts, as long as it remains accessible, hasn't been altered, and retains its metadata (like creation date and origin).

title: Audit of documents, etc., maintained in electronic form
text: Where in any law for the time being in force, there is a provision for audit of documents, records or information, that provision shall also be applicable for audit of documents, records or information processed and maintained in the electronic form.
plain_english_summary: Any laws that require an audit of business documents apply equally to documents that are kept entirely digitally.

title: Publication of rule, regulation, etc., in Electronic Gazette
text: Where any law provides that any rule, regulation, order, bye-law, notification or any other matter shall be published in the Official Gazette, then, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such rule, regulation, order, bye-law, notification or any other matter is published in the Official Gazette or Electronic Gazette...
plain_english_summary: Allows the government to publish official rules and laws digitally in an 'Electronic Gazette' instead of exclusively printing them on paper.

title: Sections 6, 7 and 8 not to confer right to insist document should be accepted in electronic form
text: Nothing contained in sections 6, 7 and 8 shall confer a right upon any person to insist that any Ministry or Department of the Central Government or the State Government or any authority or body established by or under any law or controlled or funded by the Central or State Government should accept, issue, create, retain and preserve any document in the form of electronic records or effect any monetary transaction in the electronic form.
plain_english_summary: While electronic documents are legally valid, citizens cannot force a government department to accept digital forms or payments if that specific department's systems aren't set up for it yet.

title: Power to make rules by Central Government in respect of electronic signature
text: The Central Government may, for the purposes of this Act, by rules, prescribe- (a) the type of electronic signature; (b) the manner and format in which the electronic signature shall be affixed; (c) the manner or procedure which facilitates identification of the person affixing the electronic signature; (d) control processes and procedures to ensure adequate integrity, security and confidentiality...
plain_english_summary: The Central Government has the authority to make rules dictating exactly how electronic signatures must be formatted, verified, and secured.

title: Validity of contracts formed through electronic means
text: Where in a contract formation, the communication of proposals, the acceptance of proposals, the revocation of proposals and acceptances, as the case may be, are expressed in electronic form or by means of an electronic records, such contract shall not be deemed to be unenforceable solely on the ground that such electronic form or means was used for that purpose.
plain_english_summary: Critically validates digital contracts (like 'Clickwrap' or SaaS Terms of Service), stating that a contract is fully enforceable even if the entire agreement and acceptance happened electronically.

chapter_title: ATTRIBUTION, ACKNOWLEDGMENT AND DESPATCH OF ELECTRONIC RECORDS
title: Attribution of electronic records
text: An electronic record shall be attributed to the originator- (a) if it was sent by the originator himself; (b) by a person who had the authority to act on behalf of the originator in respect of that electronic record; or (c) by an information system programmed by or on behalf of the originator to operate automatically.
plain_english_summary: If an email or digital action is sent by you, sent by someone you authorized, or sent by an automated script/system you set up, you are legally held responsible as the 'originator'.

chapter_title: ATTRIBUTION, ACKNOWLEDGMENT AND DESPATCH OF ELECTRONIC RECORDS
title: Acknowledgment of receipt
text: (1) Where the originator has not stipulated that the acknowledgment of receipt of electronic record be given in a particular form or by a particular method, an acknowledgment may be given by- (a) any communication by the addressee, automated or otherwise; or (b) any conduct of the addressee, sufficient to indicate to the originator that the electronic record has been received.

sub_structure.2: (2) Where the originator has stipulated that the electronic record shall be binding only on receipt of an acknowledgment of such electronic record by him, then unless acknowledgment has been so received, the electronic record shall he deemed to have been never sent by the originator.

plain_english_summary: An acknowledgment that a digital message was received can be an automated reply or even an action taken by the receiver. If you specify that a contract is only valid once receipt is acknowledged, it holds no legal weight until that acknowledgment occurs.

chapter_title: ATTRIBUTION, ACKNOWLEDGMENT AND DESPATCH OF ELECTRONIC RECORDS
title: Time and place of despatch and receipt of electronic record
text: (1) Save as otherwise agreed to between the originator and the addressee, the despatch of an electronic record occurs when it enters a computer resource outside the control of the originator.

sub_structure.3: (3) Save as otherwise agreed to between the originator and the addressee, an electronic record is deemed to be despatched at the place where the originator has his place of business, and is deemed to be received at the place where the addressee has his place of business.

plain_english_summary: A message is considered 'sent' the moment it leaves your server. Legally, a message is treated as being sent from your primary business address and received at the recipient's primary business address, regardless of where the physical servers are located.

chapter_title: SECURE ELECTRONIC RECORDS AND SECURE ELECTRONIC SIGNATURE
title: Secure electronic record
text: Where any security procedure has been applied to an electronic record at a specific point of time, then such record shall he deemed to be a secure electronic record from such point of time to the time of verification.
plain_english_summary: Once an official security procedure (like encryption) is applied to a digital document, the law officially recognizes it as a 'secure electronic record'.

chapter_title: SECURE ELECTRONIC RECORDS AND SECURE ELECTRONIC SIGNATURE
title: Secure electronic signature
text: An electronic signature shall be deemed to be a secure electronic signature if- (i) the signature creation data, at the time of affixing signature, was under the exclusive control of signatory and no other person; and (ii) the signature creation data was stored and affixed in such exclusive manner as may be prescribed.

sub_structure.explanation: Explanation. In case of digital signature, the "signature creation data" means the private key of the subscriber.

plain_english_summary: An electronic signature is only legally deemed 'secure' if the person signing it had exclusive control over their private key (password/hardware token) when the signature was made.

chapter_title: SECURE ELECTRONIC RECORDS AND SECURE ELECTRONIC SIGNATURE
title: Security procedures and practices
text: The Central Government may, for the purposes of sections 14 and 15, prescribe the security procedures and practices: Provided that in prescribing such security procedures and practices, the Central Government shall have regard to the commercial circumstances, nature of transactions and such other related factors as it may consider appropriate.
plain_english_summary: The Central Government sets the standards for what counts as a legally 'secure' procedure, and must take commercial reality and industry transaction types into account when making these rules.

title: Short title, extent and commencement
text: This Act may be called the Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Act, 2017.

sub_structure.2: It extends to the whole of India.
sub_structure.3: It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint.

plain_english_summary: This section outlines the name of the Act, which provides compensation to Indian States for revenue lost due to the implementation of GST, and confirms it applies to all of India.

title: Definitions
text: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,-

sub_structure.c: "cess" means the goods and services tax compensation cess levied under section 8;
sub_structure.d: "compensation" means an amount, in the form of goods and services tax compensation, as determined under section 7;
sub_structure.f: "Fund" means the Goods and Services Tax Compensation Fund referred to in section 10;
sub_structure.k: "projected growth rate" means the rate of growth projected for the transition period as per section 3;
sub_structure.r: "transition period" means a period of five years from the transition date;

plain_english_summary: Defines the key terms used in the Act, such as what constitutes the compensation 'cess' (an additional tax), the 'Fund' where this money is kept, and the 'transition period' (five years).

title: Projected growth rate
text: The projected nominal growth rate of revenue subsumed for a State during the transition period shall be fourteen per cent. per annum.
plain_english_summary: For the purpose of calculating compensation, the government assumes that a State's tax revenue would have grown by exactly 14% every year if GST had not been implemented.

title: Base year
text: For the purpose of calculating the compensation amount payable in any financial year during the transition period, the financial year ending 31st March, 2016, shall be taken as the base year.
plain_english_summary: The financial year 2015-2016 is designated as the 'base year'. All future revenue loss calculations are measured against the tax revenue a state collected during this specific year.

title: Base year revenue
text: Subject to the provision of sub-sections (2), (3), (4), (5) and (6), the base year revenue for a State shall be the sum of the revenue collected by the State and the local bodies during the base year, on account of the taxes levied by the respective State or Union and net of refunds, with respect to the following taxes, imposed by the respective State or Union, which are subsumed into goods and services tax...

sub_structure.1_a: the value added tax, sales tax, purchase tax, tax collected on works contract...
sub_structure.1_b: the central sales tax levied under the Central Sales Tax Act, 1956...
sub_structure.1_c: the entry tax, octroi, local body tax...
sub_structure.1_proviso_a: Provided that the revenue collected during the base year in a State... under the following taxes shall not be included... on the sale or purchase of petroleum crude, high speed diesel, motor spirit (commonly known as petrol), natural gas, aviation turbine fuel and alcoholic liquor for human consumption;

plain_english_summary: This defines exactly which old taxes (like VAT, Octroi, and Central Sales Tax) are added together to calculate a State's 'base year revenue'. It explicitly excludes taxes collected on alcohol and petroleum products, as those were not subsumed into GST.

title: Projected revenue for any year
text: The projected revenue for any year in a State shall be calculated by applying the projected growth rate over the base year revenue of that State.

sub_structure.illustration: If the base year revenue for 2015-16 for a concerned State... is one hundred rupees, then the projected revenue for financial year 2018-19 shall be as follows- Projected Revenue for 2018-19=100 (1+14/100)^3

plain_english_summary: To find out what a State 'should' have earned in a given year, the government takes the 2015-16 base revenue and mathematically compounds it by 14% for every year that has passed.

title: Calculation and release of compensation
text: The compensation under this Act shall be payable to any State during the transition period.

sub_structure.2: The compensation payable to a State shall be provisionally calculated and released at the end of every two months period, and shall be finally calculated for every financial year after the receipt of final revenue figures...
sub_structure.3_c: the total compensation payable in any financial year shall be the difference between the projected revenue for any financial year and the actual revenue collected by a State...

plain_english_summary: States are paid their GST compensation every two months. The exact amount owed is the difference between what they actually collected in GST and their 14% 'projected revenue'. Overpayments are adjusted at the end of the year.

title: Levy and collection of cess
text: There shall be levied a cess on such intra-State supplies of goods or services or both... and such inter-State supplies of goods or services or both... for the purposes of providing compensation to the States for loss of revenue arising on account of implementation of the goods and services tax...

sub_structure.1_proviso: Provided that no such cess shall be leviable on supplies made by a taxable person who has decided to opt for composition levy under section 10 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act.
sub_structure.2: The cess shall be levied on such supplies of goods and services as are specified in column (2) of the Schedule, on the basis of value, quantity or on such basis at such rate not exceeding the rate set forth...

plain_english_summary: To fund the state payouts, the Central Government levies an extra 'Compensation Cess' on top of standard GST for specific luxury or 'sin' goods (like cars, tobacco, and aerated drinks). Businesses using the GST composition scheme do not charge this cess.

title: Power not to recover cess not levied or short levied as a result of general practice
text: Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, if the Government is satisfied that- (a) a practice was, or is, generally prevalent regarding levy of cess (including non-levy thereof) on any supply of goods or services or both; and (b) such supplies were, or are, liable to,- (i) cess, in cases where according to the said practice, cess was not, or is not being, levied... the Government may... direct that the whole of the cess payable on such supplies... shall not be required to be paid...
plain_english_summary: If businesses in an industry widely misunderstood the rules and failed to charge the cess (or charged too little) due to a general industry practice, the Government has the power to forgive the debt and not force them to back-pay the uncollected cess.

title: Returns, payments and refunds
text: Every taxable person, making a taxable supply of goods or services or both, shall- (a) pay the amount of cess as payable under this Act in such manner; (b) furnish such returns in such forms, along with the returns to be filed under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act; and (c) apply for refunds of such cess paid in such form, as may be prescribed.

sub_structure.2: For all purposes of furnishing of returns and claiming refunds... the provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act and the rules made thereunder, shall, as far as may be, apply in relation to the levy and collection of the cess...

plain_english_summary: Businesses that sell goods subject to the compensation cess must pay it, file returns for it, and claim refunds using the exact same systems and paperwork they use for their regular Central GST returns.

title: Crediting proceeds of cess to Fund
text: The proceeds of the cess leviable under section 8 and such other amounts as may be recommended by the Council, shall be credited to a non-lapsable Fund known as the Goods and Services Tax Compensation Fund, which shall form part of the public account of India and shall be utilised for purposes specified in the said section.

sub_structure.2: All amounts payable to the States under section 7 shall be paid out of the Fund.
sub_structure.3: Fifty per cent. of the amount remaining unutilised in the Fund at the end of the transition period shall be transferred to the Consolidated Fund of India as the share of Centre, and the balance fifty per cent. shall be distributed amongst the States...

plain_english_summary: All money collected from the cess goes into a dedicated, non-lapsable 'Compensation Fund'. This fund is strictly used to pay states. Any money left over after the 5-year transition period ends will be split 50/50 between the Central Government and the States.

title: Other provisions relating to cess
text: The provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, and the rules made thereunder, including those relating to assessment, input tax credit, non-levy, short-levy, interest, appeals, offences and penalties, shall, as far as may be, mutatis mutandis, apply, in relation to the levy and collection of the cess...

sub_structure.2_proviso: Provided that the input tax credit in respect of cess on supply of goods and services leviable under section 8, shall be utilised only towards payment of said cess on supply of goods and services leviable under the said section.

plain_english_summary: Standard CGST and IGST rules regarding penalties, audits, and appeals also apply to the compensation cess. Crucially, a business can only use Input Tax Credit (ITC) earned from paying cess to offset future cess liabilitiesâ€”it cannot be used to offset regular GST liabilities.

title: Power to make rules
text: The Central Government shall, on the recommendations of the Council, by notification in the Official Gazette, make rules for carrying out the provisions of this Act.
plain_english_summary: The Central Government, acting on the GST Council's advice, has the authority to create specific operational rules to enforce this Act.

title: Laying of rules before Parliament
text: Every rule made under this Act by the Central Government shall be laid, as soon as may be after it is made, before each House of Parliament, while it is in session, for a total period of thirty days...
plain_english_summary: Any new rules created under this Act must be presented to both Houses of Parliament for 30 days to allow for parliamentary review and potential modification.

title: Power to remove difficulties
text: If any difficulty arises in giving effect to the provisions of this Act, the Central Government may, on the recommendations of the Council, by order published in the Official Gazette, make such provisions, not inconsistent with the provisions of this Act, as appear to it to be necessary or expedient for removing the difficulty:

sub_structure.1_proviso: Provided that no order shall be made under this section after the expiry of five years from the commencement of this Act.

plain_english_summary: For the first five years after this Act was implemented, the Central Government had emergency powers to issue orders to resolve any unforeseen practical difficulties in executing the law.

title: Short title and commencement
text: This Act may be called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Extension to Jammu and Kashmir) Act, 2017.

sub_structure.2: It shall be deemed to have come into force on the 8th day of July, 2017.

plain_english_summary: Establishes the name of the Act which retroactively extends the Central GST laws into Jammu and Kashmir effective from July 8, 2017.

title: Extension and amendment of Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017
text: The Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017) (hereinafter referred to as the principal Act) and all rules, notifications and orders made thereunder by the Central Government are hereby extended to, and shall be in force in, the State of Jammu and Kashmir.

sub_structure.2_a: in section 1, in sub-section (2), the words "except the State of Jammu and Kashmir" shall be omitted;
sub_structure.2_c_ii: "Provided that for the State of Jammu and Kashmir, the State Bench of the Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal constituted under this Act shall be the State Appellate Tribunal constituted under the Jammu and Kashmir Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017:"

plain_english_summary: This section officially forces the main CGST Act to apply inside Jammu and Kashmir. It goes through the original CGST Act and systematically deletes the phrase 'except the State of Jammu and Kashmir' wherever it previously appeared, ensuring a unified tax code.

title: Repeal and saving
text: The Central Goods and Services Tax (Extension to Jammu and Kashmir) Ordinance, 2017 (Ord. 3 of 2017) is hereby repealed.

sub_structure.2: Notwithstanding such repeal, anything done or any action taken under the said Ordinance shall be deemed to have been done or taken under the corresponding provisions of this Act.

plain_english_summary: Before this Act was passed, the government used a temporary executive 'Ordinance' to extend GST to J&K. This section cancels that temporary Ordinance but ensures that any legal actions taken while the Ordinance was active remain valid under this new permanent Act.

title: Short title, extent and commencement
text: (1) This Act may be called the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

sub_structure.2: (2) It shall extend to the whole of India.
sub_structure.3: (3) It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint: Provided that different dates may be appointed for different provisions of this Act...

plain_english_summary: This section establishes the official name of the IGST Act and confirms that it applies uniformly across the entirety of India.

title: Definitions
text: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,-

sub_structure.6: "export of services" means the supply of any service when,- (i) the supplier of service is located in India; (ii) the recipient of service is located outside India; (iii) the place of supply of service is outside India; (iv) the payment for such service has been received... in convertible foreign exchange...
sub_structure.13: "intermediary" means a broker, an agent or any other person... who arranges or facilitates the supply of goods or services or both, or securities, between two or more persons, but does not include a person who supplies such goods or services... on his own account;
sub_structure.14: "location of the recipient of services" means,- (a) where a supply is received at a place of business for which the registration has been obtained, the location of such place of business...
sub_structure.17: "online information and database access or retrieval services" means services whose delivery is mediated by information technology over the internet or an electronic network... and includes electronic services such as,- (i) advertising on the internet; (ii) providing cloud services...

plain_english_summary: Defines vital terms for digital and cross-border businesses. Crucially for software startups, it defines 'export of services' (requiring foreign currency payment) and 'online information and database access or retrieval services' (OIDAR), which directly covers cloud computing, SaaS, and digital content delivery.

title: Appointment of officers
text: The Board may appoint such central tax officers as it thinks fit for exercising the powers under this Act.
plain_english_summary: The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs has the authority to appoint tax officers to enforce the IGST Act.

title: Authorisation of officers of State tax or Union territory tax as proper officer in certain circumstances
text: Without prejudice to the provisions of this Act, the officers appointed under the State Goods and Services Tax Act or the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act are authorised to be the proper officers for the purposes of this Act, subject to such exceptions and conditions as the Government shall... specify.
plain_english_summary: To prevent administrative overlap, State and Union Territory GST officers are legally authorized to act as IGST officers, ensuring businesses do not have to deal with multiple distinct sets of tax authorities for the same transaction.

title: Levy and collection
text: (1) Subject to the provisions of sub-section (2), there shall be levied a tax called the integrated goods and services tax on all inter-State supplies of goods or services or both, except on the supply of alcoholic liquor for human consumption... at such rates, not exceeding forty per cent., as may be notified...

sub_structure.3: (3) The Government may... specify categories of supply of goods or services or both, the tax on which shall be paid on reverse charge basis by the recipient of such goods or services...
sub_structure.5: (5) The Government may... specify categories of services, the tax on inter-State supplies of which shall be paid by the electronic commerce operator if such services are supplied through it...
sub_structure.5_proviso: Provided that where an electronic commerce operator does not have a physical presence in the taxable territory, any person representing such electronic commerce operator... shall be liable to pay tax...

plain_english_summary: This is the core charging section. It imposes IGST (up to a maximum of 40%) on all interstate transactions. It also introduces the 'reverse charge mechanism' (where the buyer pays the tax instead of the seller) and strictly holds e-commerce platforms liable for paying the tax on services provided through their apps or websites.

title: Power to grant exemption from tax
text: (1) Where the Government is satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, it may, on the recommendations of the Council, by notification, exempt generally, either absolutely or subject to such conditions as may be specified therein, goods or services or both of any specified description from the whole or any part of the tax leviable thereon...

sub_structure.2: (2) Where the Government is satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, it may... by special order in each case, under circumstances of an exceptional nature to be stated in such order, exempt from payment of tax any goods or services or both on which tax is leviable.

plain_english_summary: The Central Government holds the power to exempt specific goods or services from IGST if it benefits the public interest, either by issuing broad notifications or special case-by-case orders.

title: Power not to recover Goods and Services Tax not levied or short-levied as a result of general practice
text: Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, if the Government is satisfied that- (a) a practice was, or is, generally prevalent regarding levy of integrated tax (including non-levy thereof) on any supply of goods or services or both; and (b) such supplies were, or are, liable to- (i) integrated tax, in cases where according to the said practice, integrated tax was not, or is not being, levied... the Government may... direct that the whole of the integrated tax payable on such supplies... shall not be required to be paid...
plain_english_summary: If an entire industry misunderstood the tax laws and failed to charge the correct IGST due to a widespread standard practice, the Government has the special power to waive the collection of those back taxes so businesses aren't unfairly crippled.

title: Inter-State supply
text: (1) Subject to the provisions of section 10, supply of goods, where the location of the supplier and the place of supply are in- (a) two different States; (b) two different Union territories; or (c) a State and a Union territory, shall be treated as a supply of goods in the course of inter-State trade or commerce.

sub_structure.2: (2) Supply of goods imported into the territory of India, till they cross the customs frontiers of India, shall be treated to be a supply of goods in the course of inter-State trade or commerce.
sub_structure.4: (4) Supply of services imported into the territory of India shall be treated to be a supply of services in the course of inter-State trade or commerce.
sub_structure.5_b: (5) Supply of goods or services or both,- ... (b) to or by a Special Economic Zone developer or a Special Economic Zone unit... shall be treated to be a supply of goods or services or both in the course of inter-State trade or commerce.

plain_english_summary: Legally defines an 'inter-state' supply. For startups, it is vital to know that all imported services (like paying a foreign company for SaaS tools) and all transactions with Special Economic Zones (SEZs) are automatically treated as inter-state transactions liable for IGST, regardless of physical location.

title: Intra-State supply
text: (1) Subject to the provisions of section 10, supply of goods where the location of the supplier and the place of supply of goods are in the same State or same Union territory shall be treated as intra-State supply:

sub_structure.1_proviso: Provided that the following supply of goods shall not be treated as intra-State supply, namely:- (i) supply of goods to or by a Special Economic Zone developer or a Special Economic Zone unit; (ii) goods imported into the territory of India till they cross the customs frontiers of India...
sub_structure.2: (2) Subject to the provisions of section 12, supply of services where the location of the supplier and the place of supply of services are in the same State or same Union territory shall be treated as intra-State supply...

plain_english_summary: Defines an 'intra-state' supply (where a transaction occurs entirely within one state). It reiterates the exception that even if an SEZ is in the same city as the supplier, transactions with it can never be considered intra-state.

title: Supplies in territorial waters
text: Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act,- (a) where the location of the supplier is in the territorial waters, the location of such supplier; or (b) where the place of supply is in the territorial waters, the place of supply, shall, for the purposes of this Act, be deemed to be in the coastal State or Union territory where the nearest point of the appropriate baseline is located.
plain_english_summary: If a transaction or supply takes place out in the ocean within India's territorial waters, the law assigns that transaction to whichever Indian state or Union territory's coastline is geographically closest.

chapter_title: PLACE OF SUPPLY OF GOODS OR SERVICES OR BOTH
title: Place of supply of goods other than supply of goods imported into, or exported from India
text: (1) The place of supply of goods, other than supply of goods imported into, or exported from India, shall be as under,- (a) where the supply involves movement of goods... the place of supply of such goods shall be the location of the goods at the time at which the movement of goods terminates for delivery to the recipient;

sub_structure.1_b: (b) where the goods are delivered by the supplier to a recipient or any other person on the direction of a third person... it shall be deemed that the said third person has received the goods and the place of supply of such goods shall be the principal place of business of such person;
sub_structure.1_c: (c) where the supply does not involve movement of goods... the place of supply shall be the location of such goods at the time of the delivery to the recipient;

plain_english_summary: This rule determines where a domestic sale of physical goods actually takes place for tax purposes. If the goods are shipped, the 'place of supply' is the final delivery destination.

chapter_title: PLACE OF SUPPLY OF GOODS OR SERVICES OR BOTH
title: Place of supply of goods imported into, or exported from India
text: The place of supply of goods,- (a) imported into India shall be the location of the importer; (b) exported from India shall be the location outside India.
plain_english_summary: A straightforward rule for cross-border goods: If a startup imports hardware, the place of supply is their office in India. If they export hardware, the place of supply is the foreign country.

chapter_title: PLACE OF SUPPLY OF GOODS OR SERVICES OR BOTH
title: Place of supply of services where location of supplier and recipient is in India
text: (1) The provisions of this section shall apply to determine the place of supply of services where the location of supplier of services and the location of the recipient of services is in India.

sub_structure.2_a: (a) made to a registered person shall be the location of such person;
sub_structure.2_b: (b) made to any person other than a registered person shall be,- (i) the location of the recipient where the address on record exists; and (ii) the location of the supplier of services in other cases.
sub_structure.12: (12) The place of supply of banking and other financial services, including stock broking services to any person shall be the location of the recipient of services on the records of the supplier of services:

plain_english_summary: Determines the 'place of supply' for services happening within India. The golden rule is: If the buyer is a GST-registered business, the place of supply is the buyer's location. If the buyer is an unregistered consumer, it's their address on record.

chapter_title: PLACE OF SUPPLY OF GOODS OR SERVICES OR BOTH
title: Place of supply of services where location of supplier or location of recipient is outside India
text: (1) The provisions of this section shall apply to determine the place of supply of services where the location of the supplier of services or the location of the recipient of services is outside India.

sub_structure.2: (2) The place of supply of services except the services specified in sub-sections (3) to (13) shall be the location of the recipient of services...
sub_structure.12: (12) The place of supply of online information and database access or retrieval services shall be the location of the recipient of services.
sub_structure.12_explanation: Explanation. For the purposes of this sub-section, person receiving such services shall be deemed to be located in the taxable territory, if any two of the following non-contradictory conditions are satisfied, namely:- (a) the location of address presented by the recipient of services through internet is in the taxable territory; (b) the credit card... has been issued in the taxable territory; (c) the billing address...

plain_english_summary: Crucial for tech startups exporting software or services. Generally, if you sell a service to a foreign client, the place of supply is the foreign client's location (making it an export). For digital services (OIDAR), complex IP and billing address tests determine where the customer actually is.

chapter_title: PLACE OF SUPPLY OF GOODS OR SERVICES OR BOTH
title: Special provision for payment of tax by a supplier of online information and database access or retrieval services
text: (1) On supply of online information and database access or retrieval services by any person located in a non-taxable territory and received by a non-taxable online recipient, the supplier of services located in a non-taxable territory shall be the person liable for paying integrated tax on such supply of services:

sub_structure.2: (2) The supplier of online information and database access or retrieval services referred to in sub-section (1) shall, for payment of integrated tax, take a single registration under the Simplified Registration Scheme...
sub_structure.2_proviso_2: Provided further that if such supplier does not have a physical presence or does not have a representative for any purpose in the taxable territory, he may appoint a person in the taxable territory for the purpose of paying integrated tax and such person shall be liable...

plain_english_summary: If a foreign tech company (like Netflix or AWS) sells digital services/cloud storage directly to unregistered Indian consumers, the foreign company is legally forced to register in India under a simplified scheme and pay IGST on those sales.

chapter_title: PLACE OF SUPPLY OF GOODS OR SERVICES OR BOTH
title: Special provision for specified actionable claims supplied by a person located outside taxable territory
text: (1) A supplier of online money gaming... not located in the taxable territory, shall in respect of the supply of online money gaming by him to a person in the taxable territory, be liable to pay integrated tax on such supply.

sub_structure.3: (3) In case of failure to comply with provisions of sub-section (1) or sub-section (2) by the supplier of the online money gaming... any information generated, transmitted, received or hosted in any computer resource used for supply of online money gaming by such supplier shall be liable to be blocked for access by the public in such manner as specified in the said Act.

plain_english_summary: Foreign companies offering online real-money gaming to Indian users must register and pay IGST. If they refuse to comply, the Indian government has the power to ban and block their apps/websites in India.

chapter_title: REFUND OF INTEGRATED TAX TO INTERNATIONAL TOURIST
title: Refund of integrated tax paid on supply of goods to tourist leaving India
text: The integrated tax paid by tourist leaving India on any supply of goods taken out of India by him shall be refunded in such manner and subject to such conditions and safeguards as may be prescribed.

sub_structure.explanation: Explanation. For the purposes of this section, the term "tourist" means a person not normally resident in India, who enters India for a stay of not more than six months for legitimate non-immigrant purposes.

plain_english_summary: Foreign tourists who buy goods in India and take those physical goods back to their home country can claim a refund on the IGST they paid.

title: Zero rated supply
text: (1) "zero rated supply" means any of the following supplies of goods or services or both, namely:- (a) export of goods or services or both; or (b) supply of goods or services or both [for authorised operations] to a Special Economic Zone developer or a Special Economic Zone unit.

sub_structure.2: (2) Subject to the provisions of sub-section (5) of section 17 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, credit of input tax may be availed for making zero-rated supplies, notwithstanding that such supply may be an exempt supply.
sub_structure.3: (3) A registered person making zero rated supply shall be eligible to claim refund of unutilised input tax credit on supply of goods or services or both, without payment of integrated tax, under bond or Letter of Undertaking...

plain_english_summary: Crucial for tech startups: Exports and sales to Special Economic Zones (SEZs) are considered 'zero-rated' (taxed at 0%). Startups can legally claim a cash refund from the government for the GST they paid on business expenses used to create those exported products or services.

chapter_title: APPORTIONMENT OF TAX AND SETTLEMENT OF FUNDS
title: Apportionment of tax and settlement of funds
text: (1) Out of the integrated tax paid to the Central Government,- (a) in respect of inter-State supply of goods or services or both to an unregistered person... the amount of tax calculated at the rate equivalent to the central tax on similar intra-State supply shall be apportioned to the Central Government.

sub_structure.2: (2) The balance amount of integrated tax remaining in the integrated tax account in respect of the supply for which an apportionment to the Central Government has been done under sub-section (1) shall be apportioned to the,- (a) State where such supply takes place; and (b) Central Government where such supply takes place in a Union territory:

plain_english_summary: Details the internal government accounting mechanism for how the Central Government takes the IGST collected from businesses and shares it with the specific State Governments where the goods or services were actually consumed.

chapter_title: APPORTIONMENT OF TAX AND SETTLEMENT OF FUNDS
title: Transfer of certain amounts
text: Where any amount has been transferred from the electronic cash ledger under this Act to the electronic cash ledger under the State Goods and Services Tax Act or the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, the Government shall transfer to the State tax account or the Union territory tax account, an amount equal to the amount transferred from the electronic cash ledger, in such manner and within such time, as may be prescribed.
plain_english_summary: Provides the legal backing for the GST Portal feature that allows businesses to seamlessly transfer their cash balances between their IGST, CGST, and SGST electronic ledgers to pay off different tax liabilities.

chapter_title: APPORTIONMENT OF TAX AND SETTLEMENT OF FUNDS
title: Transfer of input tax credit
text: On utilisation of credit of integrated tax availed under this Act for payment of,- (a) central tax in accordance with the provisions of sub-section (5) of section 49 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, the amount collected as integrated tax shall stand reduced by an amount equal to the credit so utilised and the Central Government shall transfer an amount equal to the amount so reduced from the integrated tax account to the central tax account...

sub_structure.b: (b) Union territory tax... the amount collected as integrated tax shall stand reduced by an amount equal to the credit so utilised...
sub_structure.c: (c) State tax... the amount collected as integrated tax shall stand reduced by an amount equal to the credit so utilised and shall be apportioned to the appropriate State Government...
sub_structure.explanation: Explanation. For the purposes of this Chapter, "appropriate State" in relation to a taxable person, means the State or Union territory where he is registered or is liable to be registered...

plain_english_summary: When a startup uses its accumulated IGST Input Tax Credit (ITC) to pay off its CGST, SGST, or UTGST liabilities, the government automatically transfers those funds between its internal tax accounts to settle the balance.

chapter_title: APPORTIONMENT OF TAX AND SETTLEMENT OF FUNDS
title: Tax wrongfully collected and paid to Central Government or State Government
text: (1) A registered person who has paid integrated tax on a supply considered by him to be an inter-State supply, but which is subsequently held to be an intra-State supply, shall be granted refund of the amount of integrated tax so paid in such manner and subject to such conditions as may be prescribed.

sub_structure.2: (2) A registered person who has paid central tax and State tax or Union territory tax, as the case may be, on a transaction considered by him to be an intra-State supply, but which is subsequently held to be an inter-State supply, shall not be required to pay any interest on the amount of integrated tax payable.

plain_english_summary: If a startup accidentally classifies a local sale as an interstate sale (paying IGST instead of CGST/SGST), they can get a refund. Conversely, if they accidentally paid local taxes on an interstate sale, they must pay the correct IGST, but the government waives the interest penalty for the mistake.

title: Application of provisions of Central Goods and Services Tax Act
text: Subject to the provisions of this Act and the rules made thereunder, the provisions of Central Goods and Services Tax Act relating to,- (i) scope of supply; (ii) composite supply and mixed supply... (xxv) miscellaneous provisions including the provisions relating to the imposition of interest and penalty, shall, mutatis mutandis, apply, so far as may be, in relation to integrated tax as they apply in relation to central tax as if they are enacted under this Act:

sub_structure.proviso_1: Provided that in the case of tax deducted at source, the deductor shall deduct tax at the rate of two per cent. from the payment made or credited to the supplier:
sub_structure.proviso_2: Provided further that in the case of tax collected at source, the operator shall collect tax at such rate not exceeding two per cent...
sub_structure.proviso_4: Provided also that in cases where the penalty is leviable under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act and the State Goods and Services Tax Act... the penalty leviable under this Act shall be the sum total of the said penalties.
sub_structure.proviso_5: Provided also that a maximum amount of forty crore rupees shall be payable for each appeal to be filed before the Appellate Authority or the Appellate Tribunal.

plain_english_summary: To avoid duplicating the entire rulebook, the IGST Act simply copies the rules of the main CGST Act regarding assessments, audits, penalties, and appeals. It specifies that TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) and TCS (Tax Collected at Source) for IGST are capped at 2%, and appeal deposits are capped at 40 crore rupees.

title: Import of services made on or after the appointed day
text: Import of services made on or after the appointed day shall be liable to tax under the provisions of this Act regardless of whether the transactions for such import of services had been initiated before the appointed day:

sub_structure.proviso_1: Provided that if the tax on such import of services had been paid in full under the existing law, no tax shall be payable on such import under this Act:
sub_structure.proviso_2: Provided further that if the tax on such import of services had been paid in part under the existing law, the balance amount of tax shall be payable on such import under this Act.

plain_english_summary: A transitional rule for when GST first launched: If a startup imported a service right as the GST transition happened, they had to pay IGST on it, unless they had already paid the old pre-GST taxes in full.

title: Power to make rules
text: (1) The Government may, on the recommendations of the Council, by notification, make rules for carrying out the provisions of this Act.

sub_structure.3: (3) The power to make rules conferred by this section shall include the power to give retrospective effect to the rules or any of them from a date not earlier than the date on which the provisions of this Act come into force.
sub_structure.4: (4) Any rules made under sub-section (1) may provide that a contravention thereof shall be liable to a penalty not exceeding ten thousand rupees.

plain_english_summary: The Government has the power to write specific rules to enforce the IGST Act. Interestingly, they can apply these rules retroactively (back to the launch date of GST) and can set fines of up to 10,000 rupees for breaking these specific rules.

title: Power to make regulations
text: The Board may, by notification, make regulations consistent with this Act and the rules made thereunder to carry out the provisions of this Act.
plain_english_summary: The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) can issue its own regulations to help administer the IGST law on a day-to-day basis.

title: Laying of rules, regulations and notifications
text: Every rule made by the Government, every regulation made by the Board and every notification issued by the Government under this Act, shall be laid, as soon as may be, after it is made or issued, before each House of Parliament, while it is in session, for a total period of thirty days...
plain_english_summary: Any new rules, regulations, or notifications made under this Act must be presented to both Houses of Parliament for 30 days to allow the legislature to review, modify, or cancel them.

title: Removal of difficulties
text: (1) If any difficulty arises in giving effect to any provision of this Act, the Government may, on the recommendations of the Council, by a general or a special order published in the Official Gazette, make such provisions not inconsistent with the provisions of this Act or the rules or regulations made thereunder, as may be necessary or expedient for the purpose of removing the said difficulty:

sub_structure.1_proviso: Provided that no such order shall be made after the expiry of a period of five years from the date of commencement of this Act.

plain_english_summary: For the first five years after the IGST Act was implemented, the Government had emergency powers to issue orders to fix any unforeseen practical difficulties in applying the new law.

title: Short title, extent and commencement
text: This Act may be called the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013. [cite: 60]

sub_structure.2: It extends to the whole of India. [cite: 61]
sub_structure.3: It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint. [cite: 62]

plain_english_summary: This section establishes the official name of the POSH Act and confirms that it applies across the entirety of India.

title: Definitions
text: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,- [cite: 63]

sub_structure.a: "aggrieved woman" means in relation to a workplace, a woman, of any age whether employed or not, who alleges to have been subjected to any act of sexual harassment by the respondent; [cite: 64, 65]
sub_structure.f: "employee" means a person employed at a workplace for any work on regular, temporary, ad hoc or daily wage basis... includes a co-worker, a contract worker, probationer, trainee... [cite: 79]
sub_structure.g: "employer" means in any workplace... any person responsible for the management, supervision and control of the workplace. [cite: 80, 82]
sub_structure.n: "sexual harassment" includes... physical contact and advances; or a demand or request for sexual favours; or making sexually coloured remarks; or showing pornography; or any other unwelcome physical, verbal or non-verbal conduct of sexual nature; [cite: 94, 95, 96, 97, 98, 99, 100]
sub_structure.o: "workplace" includes any private sector organisation or a private venture, undertaking, enterprise... carrying on commercial, professional... activities... [cite: 101, 103]

plain_english_summary: Defines key terms for startups, notably establishing that 'sexual harassment' covers verbal, physical, and non-verbal unwelcome conduct. It broadly defines 'employee' (including interns and contractors) and defines 'employer' as anyone managing the workplace.

title: Prevention of sexual harassment
text: No woman shall be subjected to sexual harassment at any workplace. [cite: 109]

sub_structure.2_i: implied or explicit promise of preferential treatment in her employment; or [cite: 112]
sub_structure.2_ii: implied or explicit threat of detrimental treatment in her employment; or [cite: 113]
sub_structure.2_iv: interference with her work or creating an intimidating or offensive or hostile work environment for her; or [cite: 115]
sub_structure.2_v: humiliating treatment likely to affect her health or safety. [cite: 116]

plain_english_summary: Strictly prohibits sexual harassment at work. It clarifies that harassment isn't just physical; creating a hostile work environment, threatening a woman's job, or offering promotions for sexual favors all constitute harassment.

title: Constitution of Internal Complaints Committee
text: Every employer of a workplace shall, by an order in writing, constitute a Committee to be known as the "Internal Complaints Committee": [cite: 119, 120]

sub_structure.2_a: a Presiding Officer who shall be a woman employed at a senior level at workplace from amongst the employees: [cite: 124]
sub_structure.2_b: not less than two Members from amongst employees preferably committed to the cause of women or who have had experience in social work or have legal knowledge; [cite: 127]
sub_structure.2_c: one member from amongst non-governmental organisations or associations... familiar with the issues relating to sexual harassment: [cite: 128]
sub_structure.2_proviso: Provided that at least one-half of the total Members so nominated shall be women. [cite: 129]
sub_structure.3: The Presiding Officer and every Member of the Internal Committee shall hold office for such period, not exceeding three years... [cite: 130]

plain_english_summary: Startups must legally create an Internal Complaints Committee (ICC). The ICC must be led by a senior female employee, include at least two other employees, and have one external member (like an NGO rep). At least 50% of the committee must be women.

title: Notification of District Officer
text: The appropriate Government may notify a District Magistrate or Additional District Magistrate or the Collector or Deputy Collector as a District Officer for every District to exercise powers or discharge functions under this Act. [cite: 140]
plain_english_summary: The government will appoint a District Officer (like a District Magistrate) in every district to oversee the enforcement of this Act at the local level.

title: Constitution and jurisdiction of Local Committee
text: Every District Officer shall constitute in the district concerned, a committee to be known as the "[Local Committee]" to receive complaints of sexual harassment from establishments where the [Internal Committee] has not been constituted due to having less than ten workers or if the complaint is against the employer himself. [cite: 141, 144]

sub_structure.2: The District Officer shall designate one nodal officer in every block, taluka and tehsil... to receive complaints and forward the same to the concerned [Local Committee] within a period of seven days. [cite: 145]

plain_english_summary: If a startup has fewer than 10 employees (and thus doesn't need its own ICC), or if the founder/employer is the one accused of harassment, the victim can complain directly to a government-run Local Committee.

title: Composition, tenure and other terms and conditions of Local Committee
text: The [Local Committee] shall consist of the following members to be nominated by the District Officer... [cite: 147, 148]

sub_structure.1_a: a Chairperson to be nominated from amongst the eminent women in the field of social work and committed to the cause of women; [cite: 149]
sub_structure.1_c: two Members, of whom at least one shall be a woman, to be nominated from amongst such non-governmental organisations or associations... [cite: 151]
sub_structure.2: The Chairperson and every Member of the Local Committee shall hold office for such period, not exceeding three years... [cite: 155]

plain_english_summary: Outlines the structure of the government's Local Committee, which must be led by an eminent woman in social work and include NGO representatives, serving terms of up to three years.

title: Grants and audit
text: The Central Government may, after due appropriation made by Parliament by law in this behalf, make to the State Government grants of such sums of money as the Central Government my think fit, for being utilised for the payment of fees or allowances referred to in sub-section (4) of section 7. [cite: 164, 168]

sub_structure.4: The accounts of the agency referred to in sub-section (2) shall be maintained and audited in such manner as may... be prescribed... [cite: 171]

plain_english_summary: Details how the Central Government provides funding to State Governments to pay the fees and allowances of the Local Committee members, subject to proper auditing.

title: Complaint of sexual harassment
text: Any aggrieved woman may make, in writing, a complaint of sexual harassment at workplace to the Internal Committee if so constituted, or the Local Committee... within a period of three months from the date of incident and in case of a series of incidents, within a period of three months from the date of last incident: [cite: 173]

sub_structure.1_proviso_1: where such complaint cannot be made in writing, the Presiding Officer... shall render all reasonable assistance to the woman for making the complaint in writing: [cite: 174]
sub_structure.1_proviso_2: the Internal Committee or, as the case may be, the Local Committee may, for the reasons to be recorded in writing, extend the time limit not exceeding three months... [cite: 175]
sub_structure.2: Where the aggrieved woman is unable to make a complaint on account of her physical or mental incapacity or death or otherwise, her legal heir or such other person as may be prescribed may make a complaint... [cite: 176]

plain_english_summary: A victim must file a written complaint to the company's ICC within 3 months of the harassment incident. The ICC can extend this deadline by another 3 months if needed. If the victim cannot write or is incapacitated, the committee must help her, or her legal heir can file it.

title: Conciliation
text: The Internal Committee or, as the case may be, the Local Committee, may, before initiating an inquiry under section 11 and at the request of the aggrieved woman take steps to settle the matter between her and the respondent through conciliation: [cite: 177]

sub_structure.1_proviso: Provided that no monetary settlement shall be made as a basis of conciliation. [cite: 178]
sub_structure.2: Where settlement has been arrived at... the Internal Committee... shall record the settlement so arrived and forward the same to the employer or the District Officer to take action as specified... [cite: 179]
sub_structure.4: Where a settlement is arrived at under sub-section (1), no further inquiry shall be conducted by the Internal Committee... [cite: 181]

plain_english_summary: Before a formal inquiry starts, the victim can request the ICC to try and settle the issue with the accused through conciliation. However, the company cannot use money to settle the issue at this stage. If settled, the inquiry stops.

title: Inquiry into complaint
text: Subject to the provisions of section 10, the Internal Committee or the Local Committee, as the case may be, shall, where the respondent is an employee, proceed to make inquiry into the complaint in accordance with the provisions of the service rules applicable to the respondent and where no such rules exist, in such manner as may be prescribed... [cite: 182]

sub_structure.3: For the purpose of making an inquiry under sub-section (1), the Internal Committee or the Local Committee, as the case may be, shall have the same powers as are vested in a civil court the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908... when trying a suit... [cite: 188]
sub_structure.4: The inquiry under sub-section (1) shall be completed within a period of ninety days. [cite: 192]

plain_english_summary: If conciliation fails or isn't requested, the committee must conduct a formal inquiry. During this inquiry, the committee has the same powers as a civil court (like summoning witnesses and demanding documents). The inquiry must be completed within 90 days.

title: Action during pendency of inquiry
text: During the pendency of an inquiry on a written request made by the aggrieved woman, the Internal Committee or the local Committee, as the case may be, may recommend to the employer to- [cite: 195]

sub_structure.1_a: transfer the aggrieved woman or the respondent to any other workplace; or [cite: 196]
sub_structure.1_b: grant leave to the aggrieved woman up to a period of three months; or [cite: 197]
sub_structure.1_c: grant such other relief to the aggrieved woman a may be prescribed. [cite: 198]
sub_structure.2: The leave granted to the aggrieved woman under this section shall be in addition to the leave she would be otherwise entitled. [cite: 199]
